It comes through the blood, through the years, through the watching — the long watching of a child who sees his father working, who sees his grandfather’s hands shaping something from nothing, who absorbs without knowing he is absorbing the shape of a life organized around a principle he cannot yet name.

This is the inheritance I carry. Not intellectual property. Not a brand. Not a green clay figure whose rights now belong to a corporation that appears in a convicted trafficker’s contact book. The inheritance I carry is a pattern — a way of moving through the world that was planted before I was born, tended before I understood what tending meant, and scattered by a storm that I am still learning to read.

The earlier chapters of this book described the seed-over-sword principle in the abstract. The asymmetry between the thing that creates and the thing that cuts. The patience of the dormant code against the impatience of the sharpened blade. The way empires fall and ideas survive, the way the sword wins every battle it can see and loses every war it cannot. That principle is real. It is observable. It is written into the structure of the living world with the same consistency as gravity.

But a principle means nothing until it is lived. A principle that exists only in description is a seed that never touched soil. This chapter is the soil. This chapter is the specific, particular, irreducible story of three generations who lived the seed-over-sword principle — not as a philosophy they chose but as a reality they inherited, each generation receiving the seed in a different form, each generation facing the sword from a different angle, each generation carrying the code forward through a landscape that shifted beneath their feet.

This is not a repetition of what came before. This is the principle made flesh. Three men. Three lifetimes. One seed.

The Planter

Art Clokey did not set out to prove a principle. He set out to make something beautiful.

In 1953, at the University of Southern California, studying under Slavko Vorkapich — a man who understood film as a language of pure motion and transformation — Art made a short film called Gumbasia. Abstract shapes of clay, moving to jazz music. No characters. No narrative. No commercial intent. A thesis project made by a seminary student who had also studied Buddhism, who followed Sai Baba, who named every creative act he undertook after a Sanskrit word for universal love.

Gumbasia was a seed. Not metaphorically. Structurally. It carried inside itself the complete code for everything that would follow — every character, every story, every frame of animation, every philosophical position that would emerge over the next six decades. The way a single acorn carries the complete instructions for an oak that will stand for centuries, Gumbasia carried the instructions for Gumby, for Premavision, for the entire body of work that would grow from that one strange, beautiful experiment in clay and sound.

Art did not know this. The planter rarely does. The planter plants because the seed is in his hand and the soil is before him and the act of planting is the only act that makes sense in the moment. The planter does not see the forest. The planter sees the ground, the seed, the gesture of placing one inside the other. The forest is in the code. The planter is in the moment.

From Gumbasia came Gumby. A green figure made of clay — not hard, not rigid, not fixed in any permanent form. Soft. Pliable. Capable of becoming anything, entering any story, walking through the pages of any book. A character whose very substance was transformation — because clay does not command. Clay becomes. Clay yields to the hand and then holds whatever shape the hand gives it, and the shape is not the point. The capacity to take shape is the point.

Art started with Clokey Productions — his own name. As his spiritual practice deepened, he added Premavision — the vision of universal love. He kept both names, but the addition was the tell. A sacred word, drawn from a tradition older than any entertainment industry, carrying a meaning that no executive summary could contain. The family name and the sacred word, side by side. Everything the studio produced flowed from that word. The love was not the marketing. The love was the architecture.

And here is where the seed-over-sword principle enters the specific Clokey story, not as an abstraction but as a daily practice: Art never sold.

For sixty years — from the mid-1950s until his death on January 8, 2010, at the age of eighty-eight — Art Clokey maintained control of Gumby. He had offers. He had pressure. He had decades of an industry that consolidates everything it touches telling him that independence was naivete, that control was a luxury he could not afford, that the smart move — the grown-up move, the realistic move — was to let the professionals handle distribution while he focused on the art.

He refused. Not once, not in a dramatic moment of defiance, but continuously, daily, for sixty years. The refusal was not an event. It was a practice. It was the practice of a man who understood — in his bones, in the place where understanding lives below the level of argument — that the moment he handed the seed to the sword, the sword would keep the shell and discard the life inside it. The industry would acquire the form — the green shape, the name, the merchandise potential — and strip away the Prema. Universal love does not optimize. Universal love does not scale the way quarterly earnings demand. The soul would be replaced by the strategy, and the thing that was planted would become the thing that was sold.

Art chose the seed. Every day for sixty years, he chose the seed. He stayed small. He stayed in Sausalito, running his own studio, training animators who would go on to work at Pixar and on Tim Burton’s Nightmare Before Christmas, pouring into the world a body of work that carried philosophy in its form — not in its marketing, not in its press releases, not in its investor decks, but in the clay itself. The green figure that could walk into books. The Blockheads — red, flat, geometric, rigid — who could not follow. The entire cosmology of imagination over rigidity, of life over mechanism, of the living thing over the dead machine, encoded in a children’s television show and broadcast to millions of families who received it without knowing what they were receiving.

This is what the planter does. The planter encodes the future in a form the present cannot read. The planter buries the message in the medium and trusts that time will reveal what the moment conceals. Art Clokey planted a philosophy inside a clay figure and handed it to children, and the children carried it in their imaginations for decades, and the philosophy survived inside them the way a dormant seed survives inside a dried cone — waiting for the fire, waiting for the heat, waiting for the conditions that would crack the casing and release the life inside.

The planter does not see the forest. The planter does not need to. The planter needs only to plant true — to encode the right information in the right vessel and place it in the ground with enough love that the code remains intact. Art planted true. For sixty years, the code remained intact.

That was his generation’s work. That was his gift. That was the seed.

The Tender

The second generation does not get to plant. The second generation inherits a living thing and must keep it alive in soil that has changed since the planting.

My father, Joe Clokey, became president of Premavision after Art’s death. He inherited not just the intellectual property — the legal rights, the trademarks, the licensing agreements — but the living thing those legal structures were meant to protect. He inherited the seed. And his task was not to plant a new one but to tend what had been planted, to water it, to protect it, to keep the soil from being paved over by an industry that had consolidated dramatically since Art first broadcast Gumby in 1956.

The world Art planted into and the world Joe inherited were not the same world. In Art’s era, an independent creator could reach an audience. The channels were fewer, the gatekeepers less concentrated, the industry less vertically integrated. An independent studio in Sausalito could produce content and find distribution without surrendering the seed. It was not easy — it was never easy — but it was possible.

By the time Joe took the reins, the channels had narrowed. The Telecommunications Act of 1996 had removed the caps on media ownership while keeping the licensing structure intact. Fifty media companies became six. Six companies controlled three-quarters of everything Americans saw and heard. The gatekeepers did not just control access — they controlled the terms of access. And the terms were always the same: give us the intellectual property, or we do not distribute.

Joe refused the terms. Like his father before him, he chose the seed over the sword. He would not hand the code to people who could not read it. He would not let Prema be stripped from the architecture so that the shell could be filled with whatever the market demanded this quarter. He held the line.

But holding the line cost more in his generation than it had in Art’s. Because Art was planting — Art was building something new, creating something from nothing, riding the energy of a first creation. Joe was tending — Joe was maintaining something that already existed, in an environment that had turned hostile to its existence. The planter has the momentum of creation. The tender has the weight of preservation. The planter runs downhill. The tender pushes uphill. Both are necessary. Both serve the seed. But the tender’s work is invisible in a way the planter’s is not, because the tender’s success looks like nothing changing — and nothing changing, in a world that demands constant growth, looks like failure.

Joe spent eight years trying to bring Gumby back. He pitched Classic Media — six years, no production. He approached the Jim Henson Company for a new stop-motion series faithful to Art’s vision — no co-production partner would fund it. He went to Amazon — the deal collapsed. He co-authored Gumby Imagined with my stepmother, Joan, documenting the art and philosophy behind the clay. He did everything a tender does — he watered, he pruned, he protected, he fought the drought with his own life force, carrying buckets from a well that was running dry.

The industry did not reject Joe because Gumby lacked value. The Gumby movie, released in 1995 through a tiny distributor in twenty-one theaters, sold a million copies on VHS. A million families bought it when they could find it. The audience was there. The demand was proven. What was missing was not market validation. What was missing was willingness — the willingness of the gatekeepers to distribute something they did not control.

This is the sword’s most patient weapon. The sword does not always swing. Sometimes the sword simply stands at the gate and waits. It does not attack the tender. It does not need to. It simply denies the tender access to the resources the living thing needs to grow, and it waits for the tender to exhaust himself carrying water from further and further away. The sword knows that the gardener has a finite life force. The sword knows that the man who will not sell is the man the system will try to exhaust. And exhaustion kills as surely as any blade.

My father died on March 2, 2018. Cardiac arrest. He was fifty-six years old. He had spent eight years tending a living thing in a drought, and the drought killed him. Not with a sword. With time. With rejection. With the slow grinding pressure of a system that does not reward principle, that does not recognize Prema, that cannot measure the value of something that refuses to be measured in its terms.

The tender’s work is the hardest work. The planter has the joy of creation. The third generation has the energy of the new. The tender has neither. The tender has only the weight of the thing that was planted and the knowledge that if he sets it down, it dies. The tender holds it up by holding himself up, and when the tender falls, the thing he was holding falls with him.

Joe fell. And the thing he was holding was exposed.

The Storm Between Generations

Between the planter and whatever comes next, there is always a storm. This is not tragedy. This is pattern. This is the Fibonacci principle applied to lineage — each generation rising and falling higher and harder than the one before, each fall scattering more seeds than the last, each rise reaching further than what came before it.

The storm that took Art was the storm of time. He died at eighty-eight, having held the seed for six decades, having lived long enough to see the world change around his planting without changing the planting itself. His death was the natural end of a long stewardship — grievous, yes, but expected. The tree lost its planter, but the tree was still standing.

The storm that took Joe was different. It was faster, more violent, more total. Fifty-six years old. Eight years of fighting. A body that spent itself keeping the thing alive. His death was not the natural end of a long life — it was the breaking of a man who gave more than he had. The tree lost its tender, and this time the tree had no one left to hold the sword at bay.

And so the sword moved in. The Gumby IP was acquired in February 2022. The character. The name. The distribution rights. Film, television, streaming, consumer products, licensing, publishing. The green shape that walked into books now belongs to a corporation.

Premavision and Prematoy — the companies that held the Gumby IP — were sold. The character, the name, the distribution rights, all of it. But the family kept the rights to the family history — Art’s story, Joe’s story, the Clokey lineage, the meaning behind the clay. They sold the form. They kept the stone. A corporation can animate a green shape. It cannot tell the story of why it exists.

They are making Gumby Kids. Two-dimensional animation. No clay. No stop-motion. No transformation. No philosophy. The thing that made Gumby Gumby — the tactile, handmade, living quality of clay shaped by human hands — replaced by the thing the industry does cheaply and quickly. The form survives. The soul has been extracted.

From the sword’s perspective, this is victory. The property is acquired. The brand is in the portfolio. The quarterly report can reference the IP. The form has been captured and repurposed.

But the sword does not understand what it has not taken. The sword acquired the character. It acquired Premavision and Prematoy — the companies, the names, the filings. But it did not acquire Prema. It did not acquire the family history — the sixty years of Art’s refusal, the eight years of Joe’s fight, the lineage that runs back to Charles Farrington and forward to the Living Age. It acquired the intellectual property filing. It did not acquire the intellectual property — the actual property of the intellect, the philosophy, the vision, the love that named the studio before the first frame was shot. The sword took the husk. The kernel is in the family.

This is the space between generations. The terrible, fertile, necessary space where the old form has been lost and the new form has not yet emerged. The space where the seed is in the soil and the sword believes it has won and the world has moved on to other things — and beneath the surface, in the darkness where no one is looking, the code is activating.

The Third Generation

I am the third generation. I did not choose this. You do not choose an inheritance of this kind — it chooses you, the way water chooses the lowest ground, the way roots choose the direction of moisture, the way the code inside the seed chooses the moment of germination based on conditions the seed did not create and cannot control.

I grew up watching Gumby. Not as a consumer watching a product — as a grandson watching his grandfather’s theology animated in twelve frames per second. I did not know that was what I was watching. Children never do. Children absorb the code without reading the label, the way a field absorbs rain without analyzing the water chemistry. The seed entered my soil before I knew what soil was.

I watched my father fight. I watched the meetings, the pitches, the rejections. I watched a man pour his life into a system that did not want what he was offering because what he was offering could not be controlled. I watched principle meet the machine and I watched the machine grind principle down to dust. I was fourteen when he told me he was “being affected by them the same way Jim Henson was.” I did not fully understand. I understand now.

And I carry the storm. The loss. The scattering. The grief that arrives not in a single wave but in a slow tide that rises every time I see the green figure animated without clay, distributed without love, produced without Prema. The anger — the red flame that must be constantly, deliberately, consciously converted to green — at a system that exhausted my father and acquired my grandfather’s creation and called it business.

But here is what the seed-over-sword principle says about the third generation, and this is the part that the sword cannot hear: the third generation does not tend the old tree. The old tree is gone. The third generation grows a forest.

The first generation proves the principle. Art proved that one man, one lifetime, full creative control, full philosophical alignment, no sale, no compromise could produce something real. He proved the seed could grow true in hostile soil. That was his generation’s work.

The second generation proves the cost. Joe proved that holding the line in a captured system requires everything you have and more than you have. He proved that the tender’s work is real work, that principle is not a metaphor but a practice with a price, and that the price is paid in the currency of a human life. That was his generation’s work.

The third generation plants the forest. Not one tree in one place tended by one gardener — a distributed system, a network of growth, an architecture so decentralized that no single acquisition can capture it, no single loss can end it, no single storm can do anything but scatter it further.

Art needed NBC’s permission to reach an audience. Joe needed the industry’s permission. I do not need anyone’s permission. The internet is the first distribution system in human history that does not require a government license. Open-source code cannot be acquired because it belongs to everyone. Biology — the living architecture I am building, the genome design systems, the structures that grow and self-repair and adapt — cannot be controlled by a corporation because life does not answer to shareholders.

The tools changed. The principle did not. The seed is still the seed. The sword is still the sword. But the soil — the soil is everywhere now, and the sword cannot pave it all.

The Bridge

This chapter is a bridge. It stands between the principles described in the early chapters of this book and the specific family story that occupies the chapters that follow. It is the hinge — the place where the abstract becomes particular, where the pattern meets the people, where the seed-over-sword principle stops being a description of how the world works and starts being a description of how three specific lives were lived and lost and carried forward.

What follows is the full story. The chapters ahead will tell you about Art in detail — what he built, why he built it, what the clay meant, what Prema meant, what it looked like to hold the line for sixty years in an industry designed to break you. They will tell you about Joe — what he carried, what he fought, what the system did to the man who would not sell, what it costs to be a tender in a drought. They will tell you about the storm — what was lost, what was scattered, what was carried by the wind to soil that no one prepared.

And they will tell you about what grows now. The Living Age. The forest that is emerging from the scattered seeds. The architecture that carries the philosophy not in a single character that can be acquired but in a system that cannot be acquired because it was designed, from its foundation, to belong to everyone.

The seed over the sword. Not as a principle. As a family. As three generations. As a grandfather who planted, a father who tended, a son who is learning what grows from the scattered seeds.

Art encoded the future in clay. Joe protected the code with his life. The storm carried the code beyond the reach of both of them. And the code — the actual information, the Prema, the philosophy of imagination over rigidity and love over commerce and the living thing over the dead machine — the code is germinating now. In new soil. In new forms. In a medium the planter never imagined and the tender never lived to see.

The seed does not need the tree to survive. The seed is the information itself. And the information, scattered by the storm, is growing in places the sword cannot find, in forms the sword cannot recognize, on a timetable the sword cannot control.

This is the Clokey story. This is the generational arc. This is the seed over the sword — not in theory, but in blood.

Art planted the seed. Joe tended the tree. The storm scattered both.

The sword took the form. The code survived in the family.

Three generations. One principle. The forest is growing.

Chapter 15: The Names That Wrote Themselves

Book I: The Spiral Seed — Part Four: The Clokey Line

The Discovery

There is a tradition, older than any institution, that names carry meaning — that the word spoken over a life shapes what that life becomes. In Hebrew scripture, God renames Abram to Abraham, Jacob to Israel, Simon to Peter. The name is not a label. It is a commission. A word spoken before the story begins, encoding the story’s arc into a single sound.

The Clokey family did not plan this. No one sat down and designed a naming system that would, across generations and marriages and branches, spell out a unified vision for living architecture, voluntary order, and biological design. And yet — when you lay the names out and read what they mean — they say exactly that.

Every name in the family points to the same element: earth. Every name describes the same work: tending living things on a foundation of stone. Every name prophesies the same project — the one now called the Living Age.

The names wrote themselves.

The Foundation: Clokey

The family name comes from Irish Gaelic cloch — stone. Rock. Bedrock. The earth element at its deepest and most immovable.

Stone does not react. Stone does not chase. Stone waits. It holds its ground while everything above it shifts — weather, seasons, empires, industries. The clay on top can be shaped, sold, transferred, rebranded. The stone beneath it never moves.

Art Clokey spent his life shaping clay — soft earth, pliable, transformable. He made a green figure that walked into books and showed children that the world changes through imagination. Freedom through transformation. Art named his studio Premavision — Sanskrit for “Universal Love.” He held Gumby his entire life. He never sold. He understood that the worst outcome is watching something you created go a direction you never intended under someone else’s hands.

In 2022, the clay was sold. Gumby was acquired — the shaped form, the character, the intellectual property. They bought what sat on top of the stone. They did not buy the stone. They cannot. Cloch does not transfer. The bedrock stays with the land it belongs to.

Jesus said it in Matthew 7: “Everyone who hears these words of mine and puts them into practice is like a wise man who built his house on the rock.” A sage who built his house on Clokey. It was already written.

The Birth Name: Farrington

Art Clokey was not born a Clokey. He was born Arthur Charles Farrington.

Arthur — from Celtic artos, the bear. But Arthur in legend is not defined by strength. He is defined by return. Rex quondam, rexque futurus — the once and future king. The one who falls at Camlann, is carried to Avalon, and comes back when the land needs him. Arthur is the king whose reign is not continuous but cyclical — he goes away and he returns.

Charles — from Germanic karl, meaning free man. Not a serf, not a subject, not a dependent of any institution. A sovereign individual.

Farrington — from Old English fearn (fern) + tun (enclosure, settlement). A settlement among ferns. A living shelter.

Art Clokey’s birth name: the returning king, a free man, in a living shelter.

His birth name described exactly what he would spend his life creating — living things, green things, growing things that shelter the imagination. Gumby was a fern enclosure. A green space where children could enter and be protected by a world that transformed around them. Farrington.

When he was adopted, he received the name Clokey — stone. The birth name described the vision. The adopted name gave it a foundation. The fern enclosure, built on rock.

The Grandmothers

Ruth (Parkander) Clokey

Ruth — Hebrew, meaning companion, friend, woman of loyalty. But the name is the book. In scripture, Ruth is the Moabite woman — the foreigner — who refuses to leave her mother-in-law Naomi after both their husbands die. “Where you go I will go. Your people will be my people and your God my God.” She enters the royal lineage of Israel not by blood but by voluntary loyalty — by choosing to stay when the world told her to leave. Ruth becomes the great-grandmother of King David. The line of kings begins with a woman who chose.

Parkander — Swedish. Park (enclosed ground) + -ander (from Greek andros, man). The steward of the kept ground. The one who tends the enclosed living space. Not the owner — the keeper. The one whose role is not dominion but cultivation.

Parkander is the Spiral Steward. The same word, centuries earlier, in a different language. The philosophy Sage would arrive at through bioinformatics, Hayek, and the study of spontaneous order — his grandmother already carried it in her maiden name.

Ruth Parkander Clokey: the loyal companion, steward of the kept ground, on stone. The woman who stays, tends, and holds the foundation.

Joan (Helmstein) Landis

Joan — from Hebrew Yohanan, meaning God is gracious. The feminine form of John — the name of the Baptist, the one at the Jordan, the one who baptizes with water and says someone greater is coming.

Helmstein — German. Helm (protection, shelter) + Stein (stone). Protected stone. The stone that shelters. The -stein suffix is one of the strongest markers of Ashkenazi Jewish heritage in German surnames — families who carried the earth in their names across centuries of European diaspora.

Landis — German. Land — earth, ground, territory. The land itself. Another surname common in Ashkenazi Jewish families from German-speaking regions.

Joan Helmstein Landis: God’s grace, the protected stone, of the land. Grace held in stone, rooted in the earth.

What the Grandmothers Carry

Grandmother Name Meaning
Ruth (Parkander) Clokey Loyal companion, steward of the ground, on stone The choice that begins the royal line
Joan (Helmstein) Landis God’s grace, protected stone, of the land The grace that shelters the foundation

One carries loyalty — the voluntary bond that creates lineage. The other carries grace — the divine protection that preserves it. Between them, the two grandmothers hold the foundation from both sides: Ruth tends the stone. Joan shelters it.

The Josephs

The name Joseph appears across the family like a thread woven through every branch.

Joseph — Hebrew, Yosef, meaning “he will add” or “God will increase.” In Genesis, Joseph is the son who is sold by his brothers into a foreign land, stripped of everything, descending into prison — and through that descent, he rises to feed the nations and save the family that rejected him. The pattern: betrayal, exile, descent, and then — God increases through him.

Joseph Parkander — God will increase through the steward of the ground.

Joseph Walden Clokey — Walden from Old English weald, meaning forest, woodland, the wild living place. Thoreau went to Walden to find truth by leaving the institution and entering the living world. God will increase through the forest on stone.

Joseph Arthur Clokey — God will increase through the returning king on stone.

Three Josephs. Three branches. Three times the same promise: what is cast down will rise. What is sold into foreign hands will become the means of salvation. What descends will increase.

Gumby was sold. The family lost the clay. And through that loss — through the descent — the Living Age grows. New clay on the same stone. God increases.

The Children of Stone

Art and Ruth Clokey’s grandchildren carry the pattern into the third generation:

Shasta Joseph Clokey

Shasta — Mount Shasta, the sacred volcanic peak of northern California. A dormant volcano — fire held inside stone. The mountain that looks still on the surface but carries molten energy within. Sacred to the native peoples of the region as a place where the spirit world meets the physical.

A volcano is the earth element containing fire. Stone holding flame. Dormant does not mean dead. It means waiting.

Joseph — God will increase.

Clokey — Stone.

The sacred mountain with fire inside, through whom God will increase, on stone. Fire waiting within the earth for the moment of release.

Sequoia Jo Ana Rose Clokey

Sequoia — the largest living things on earth. Giant redwoods that grow for thousands of years, that survive fire because their bark is fire-resistant. The forest that endures everything. And the name comes from Sequoyah, the Cherokee scholar who gave his people a written language — a single man who didn’t conquer or rule but gave his people the power of the word.

Jo — Joseph. God will increase.

Ana — from Hebrew Hannah, meaning grace, favor.

Rose — the flower that grows from thorns. In Judges 9, Jotham warns that only the thornbush accepts the crown. But the rose grows from the thorn. The living thing that transforms what is sharp into what is beautiful.

Clokey — Stone.

The living thing that endures fire, through whom God’s grace increases, the flower from thorns, on stone. The tree that stands for millennia. The word made visible. The thorn redeemed.

Sage Arthur Jordan Clokey

Sage — from Latin sapere, to taste, to perceive, to know directly. Not book knowledge — the wisdom that recognizes truth on contact, the way a tongue recognizes sweetness. The serpent’s gift — seeing clearly.

Arthur — the returning king. The third Arthur in the Clokey line. Rex quondam, rexque futurus.

Jordan — from Hebrew Yarden, meaning the descender. The river that flows down — from Mount Hermon to the Dead Sea, the lowest point on earth. The Jordan is where Jesus was baptized. The dove descended there. The river that goes to the lowest place so that what passes through it can rise.

Clokey — Stone.

The one who sees clearly, the king who returns, descending to the lowest place, on stone. The sage who goes down before he goes up, built on what cannot be moved.

The Three Together

Child Name meaning Element
Shasta Joseph Clokey Mountain with fire inside — waiting on stone Earth holding fire
Sequoia Jo Ana Rose Clokey Tree that survives fire — grace from thorns on stone Earth enduring fire
Sage Arthur Jordan Clokey The one who sees — returning king — descending — on stone Earth, water, wisdom

A mountain. A tree. A sage. Three children named for things that endure — the peak that holds fire, the tree that outlasts it, and the wisdom that sees through it. All rooted in stone. All carrying Joseph — the promise that God will increase through what was cast down.

The Pattern

Lay the names out as a single text and read them in sequence:

Name Meaning
Farrington The living shelter — settlement among ferns
Clokey Stone — the bedrock
Parkander Steward of the kept ground — the Spiral Steward
Ruth The loyal companion who enters the royal line by choice
Helmstein Protected stone — the stone that shelters
Landis Land — the earth itself
Joseph God will increase — what is cast down will rise
Walden The forest — the wild living place
Arthur The returning king
Charles Free man
Shasta The mountain with fire inside
Sequoia The tree that endures — the word made visible
Sage The one who sees
Jordan The descender — the river, the baptism
Rose The flower from thorns

Every name is the earth element. Every name describes either the foundation (stone, rock, land), the living thing that grows from it (fern, forest, sequoia, rose), or the one who tends it (steward, farmer, sage). No name in the family points to empire, conquest, industry, or domination. No name means king in the worldly sense — even Arthur is the king who returns, who serves the round table, who falls and comes back. These are names of ground, growth, endurance, and stewardship.

The family was named for the Living Age before the Living Age had a name.

What the Names Say

Read together, the Clokey family names are a single statement:

Build a living shelter on stone. Tend the ground. Stay loyal to the foundation. Let God increase through what is cast down. Carry the fire inside the mountain. Stand like the tree that survives the burning. See clearly. Descend before you rise. And when the time comes — return.

This is the Living Age.

Not a business plan. Not a startup. Not a technology company. A commission written in names before anyone alive could read it.

Farrington — the living shelter. That is what living architecture builds. Parkander — the steward of the ground. That is what the Spiral Steward does. Clokey — stone. That is what the foundation is. Joseph — God will increase. That is the promise. Arthur — the king returns. That is the arc. Sage — the one who sees. That is who reads it. Jordan — the descender. That is the path.

The names wrote themselves. The Living Age is just learning to read them.

Chapter 16: Art and the Green Knight

The Orphan

He was born Arthur Charles Farrington on October 12, 1921, in Detroit, Michigan. His father left before the boy could form a memory of him. His mother could not keep him. By the age of nine, Art was an orphan.

I have thought about this more than almost anything else in the Clokey story – more than the fame, more than the legacy, more than the green clay figure that became an American icon. I think about the orphaning. Because everything that followed grew from this wound, the way a tree grows from a fracture in stone – not despite the break but through it, using the crack as the only path toward light. Every act of creation Art Clokey ever performed was, at its root, a response to having been abandoned by the people who were supposed to hold him. Everything he built was shelter. Everything he imagined was a world where a small, flexible, green figure could walk into any story and belong.

An orphan at nine. Moved through the foster care system the way the system moves children – as a problem to be managed, not a soul to be cultivated. A boy without a name that stuck, without a home that held, without the single thing every child needs more than food or shelter: the knowledge that someone will not leave. And then a rescue. Joseph Waddell Clokey – a composer, a man of music and gentleness – adopted the boy. Gave him a name. Gave him a home. Gave him the thing the system could not provide: a father who stayed.

Art Clokey was shaped by two forces, and they were the two forces that shape everyone: the wound and the healing. The wound was abandonment – the knowledge, seared into his childhood, that the world does not owe you belonging. That the people who are supposed to stay can leave. That the structure you depend on can dissolve without warning, without explanation, without regard for the child standing in the wreckage. This knowledge does one of two things to a person. It makes you desperate to belong to any structure that will have you – to join, to comply, to become whatever the nearest institution demands so that you are never again the one without a place. Or it makes you determined to build a structure so real, so true, so rooted in something deeper than convenience or commerce, that it cannot abandon you. Because you are the one who made it. Because the love in its foundation is your love. Because no one can take what you refuse to hand over.

Art became the second kind. He became the builder. And the thing he built – the green figure, the studio, the fifty-seven years of uncompromised independence – was the orphan’s answer to the world that had left him. You left me. I will build something that does not leave. Something alive, something green, something that can walk into any story because it was never locked inside a single one.

The other force was Joseph Clokey – the adoptive father, the composer, the man who demonstrated by the shape of his life that creation is not commerce. That music exists because beauty exists. That a man can spend his life making something that does not sell, and the making is still worth the life. Joseph did not teach Art to be an artist. Joseph showed Art what an artist looked like – quiet, devoted, making music because music was the truest thing he knew. And Art spent the rest of his life becoming one. Not a commercial artist. Not a studio executive who happened to animate. An artist. A man whose work was an extension of his inner life, and whose inner life was a search for the love that the world had failed to provide and that he would spend sixty years pouring into clay.

I write about my grandfather from a distance. I was five when he died. I do not have the personal memories my father had – the studio visits, the long conversations, the accumulated weight of decades spent in the orbit of a man who was both a father and a cultural monument. I know Art Clokey through the legacy, through the public record, through the stories my father told me, through the work itself. I know him the way you know a mountain from the valley – by its shape against the sky, by the rivers that flow from it, by the way the land around it has been formed by its presence. I cannot tell you what his hands felt like or what his voice sounded like when he was tired. I can tell you what he built, and what he refused to let anyone destroy, and what that refusal cost, and what it means now that he is gone.

The Education

He studied at Hartford Art School and Pomona College before arriving at the University of Southern California for film school. There he found the teacher who gave him the technical language for what he had always felt.

Slavko Vorkapich was a Serbian filmmaker and montage theorist who understood cinema not as a commercial medium but as a language – a language of movement, rhythm, and transformation. Vorkapich taught that film was not about recording reality. Film was about revealing reality – showing the dynamics beneath the surface, the forces that move through matter, the aliveness of the physical world that the naked eye registers but the conscious mind rarely names. Under Vorkapich, a student did not learn to make movies. A student learned to see – to perceive the dance inside the stillness, the motion inside the form, the becoming inside the being.

Under Vorkapich, Art made Gumbasia in 1953. A short film. Abstract clay shapes moving to jazz – stretching, compressing, pulsing, transforming. No characters. No story. No commercial intent. Just clay being alive. Moving the way music moves. Changing the way living things change – not in straight lines but in rhythms, in waves, in the spiraling, breathing pattern of matter that has been touched by something that animates it from within.

Gumbasia was a thesis film. It was also a Genesis. The entire Gumby universe – the clay, the transformation, the idea that form is not fixed, that the living thing can change and grow and enter any story – was encoded in those few minutes of abstract movement. The way a single acorn carries the complete instructions for a tree that will stand for centuries, Gumbasia carried the instructions for everything that would follow. The seed was planted in a university film lab, and the code inside it would take sixty years to fully express.

Art showed Gumbasia to the Fox movie studios. They saw what he had done with clay and music and they asked him to make a character out of it. From that conversation came the idea of a figure made of the same transformable clay. Green – the color of growth, of chlorophyll, of every living thing that converts light into life. His form was a sprouting seedling: legs like roots, arms like branches, head leaning toward the sun. Soft, pliable, capable of stretching into any shape without breaking. And the figure could walk into books – literally step through the pages into other stories, other worlds, other possibilities. Imagination was the doorway. Not a product. A portal.

Gumby was born in 1955. Art then bought the character back from the studio – he understood, even then, that the living thing had to stay in the hands of the person who understood what it was. He took Gumby to NBC, where the character found his audience on The Howdy Doody Show and then his own series. And with Gumby, Art Clokey found the form that would carry his philosophy for the rest of his life.

The Philosophy

Art Clokey was a man on a spiritual search that never ended and never narrowed.

He started in the Episcopal church – the adopted boy seeking structure, seeking the father, seeking the institution that would not leave. He studied at Hartford Theological Seminary. He learned the rigor of systematic theology, the discipline of thinking about God in organized categories, the weight of a tradition that stretches back two thousand years. But the institution could not hold him, because Art was not looking for an institution. He was looking for God. And God, as Art would discover over and over throughout his life, does not live inside the walls that institutions build to contain Him.

In the 1960s, the counterculture opened doors that the seminary had kept closed. Art explored Buddhism. He sat in meditation. He studied Eastern philosophy – not as an academic exercise or a fashionable dalliance, but as a man genuinely trying to understand the structure of consciousness, the nature of compassion, the relationship between the inner life and the outer form. He was trying to understand why the world abandons its children, and what force could make a man spend his life building shelters for children the world has not abandoned yet.

And then he found Sathya Sai Baba – the Indian guru who taught universal love as the foundation of all spiritual practice. Prema. The Sanskrit word that would name the studio. The word that would fund every creative decision Art ever made. Not fund in the financial sense – Art was never wealthy, never commercially successful in the way the industry measures success. Fund in the architectural sense. Prema was the foundation on which every frame of animation rested. The root system beneath the clay. The reason the clay was green and not gray, alive and not mechanical, free and not controlled.

He added a second name to the studio – Premavision. The vision of universal love. He kept Clokey Productions alongside it, but the addition was not a marketing decision. This was a man encoding his deepest conviction into the name of the thing he was building, the way a father names a child – not to describe what the child is, but to speak into existence what the child is meant to become. Premavision was the word spoken over the work before the work began. Every episode, every character, every story that emerged from that studio emerged from under that word. The love was not the branding. The love was the architecture.

I want to be careful here, because the tendency of the modern mind is to flatten this journey into a caricature – to call Art a “spiritual seeker” the way the culture calls people “spiritual but not religious,” as though the seeking were a hobby rather than a discipline, a phase rather than a lifetime. Art’s spiritual journey was not dilettantism. It was rigorous. It was costly. It was the organizing principle of his entire creative life.

He studied at an Episcopal seminary and learned the structure of Christian theology. He practiced Buddhist meditation and learned the discipline of inner silence. He followed Sai Baba and learned the power of Prema as a structural principle – not sentiment, not greeting-card love, but love as the force that organizes the universe, the way gravity organizes matter. And he poured all of it – every tradition, every practice, every hard-won insight from decades of genuine seeking – into the clay.

Davey and Goliath was explicitly Christian – a clay-animated series produced for the Lutheran Church in America, telling stories of faith, conscience, and moral courage for children who needed to hear that the world has a moral structure and that courage matters. Gumby was implicitly spiritual – a green figure who could enter any story, who was soft enough to change but strong enough to return to his own shape, whose enemies were the Blockheads: red, flat, geometric, rigid, incapable of imagination or transformation. Two shows. Two expressions. One source. The man was not confused. The man was comprehensive. He drew from every well because he was building something that was meant to hold water from all of them.

He said it plainly. He called Gumby “an act of love to children.” He refused to license the character for merchandising for eight years after creating him. Eight years. In an industry that measures time in quarterly reports, a man sat on a beloved, recognizable, massively popular children’s character for eight years because he did not want to exploit the children who loved it. “I was a very idealistic person,” he said, “and I didn’t want to exploit children.”

That sentence. Read it again. Let it sit against the backdrop of an industry where the woman who ran the dominant children’s television network for twelve years appears in a convicted child trafficker’s contact book. Let it sit against the backdrop of a system where the man she hired had unsupervised access to child actors for twenty-five years. Let it sit against the backdrop of an industry where every independent creator who refused to surrender was fired, starved, bought out, or taken over.

“I didn’t want to exploit children.”

Art Clokey was not just an animator. He was a standing rebuke. His existence – his persistence, his independence, his refusal to sell – was an indictment of every practice the industry depended on. The industry monetized children’s attention. Art protected it. The industry extracted value from the trust between a child and a screen. Art deposited value into that trust. The industry treated children’s entertainment as a pipeline to consumer behavior. Art treated it as a sacred space where imagination could grow without being harvested.

The Green Knight

In the medieval poem Sir Gawain and the Green Knight, a massive green figure rides into King Arthur’s court on New Year’s Day and issues a challenge. He offers any knight the chance to strike him with his own axe – on the condition that the knight must find him one year later and receive the same blow.

Sir Gawain accepts. He cuts off the Green Knight’s head. The Green Knight picks up his head, reminds Gawain of the bargain, and rides away.

The Green Knight is not an enemy in the conventional sense. He does not seek to conquer Camelot or overthrow Arthur. He is a test. He rides in with an impossible challenge and watches to see who has the courage to accept it and the honor to fulfill it. The entire poem is about one question: will the knight keep his word, even when keeping it costs him everything? The Green Knight is nature itself, walking into the court and asking: are you worthy of what you claim to be?

Art Clokey was the Green Knight of children’s entertainment.

He rode into the industry with something impossible – a green clay figure in a world moving toward hand-drawn cel animation and then toward computer-generated imagery. A character built on spiritual principles in an industry built on commercial ones. A studio named after a Sanskrit word for universal love in a marketplace that spoke only the language of quarterly returns. His mere existence was the challenge. Not in words. In being. The challenge was: can you deal with someone who will not sell? Can your industry accommodate a creator who maintains full ownership, full control, full alignment between the vision and the product? Can you work with a man whose bottom line is not money but meaning?

The industry could not.

Not because Art was difficult. By all accounts he was generous, warm, eccentric in the way that genuinely creative people are eccentric – the kind of eccentric that comes from spending more time in your inner world than in the social world, from caring more about the shape of the clay than the shape of the deal. But the industry’s architecture is the sword’s architecture. Consolidation. Acquisition. The conversion of art into intellectual property and intellectual property into revenue streams. The industry does not know what to do with a creator who will not convert. The industry’s entire machinery runs on the assumption that everything is for sale – and when it encounters something that is not, the machinery grinds and stalls and eventually routes around the obstruction the way a river routes around a stone it cannot move.

He trained more than half the animators who worked on Tim Burton’s Nightmare Before Christmas. His people went to Pixar. They went to Disney. They went into the industry and shaped it from the inside. Art stayed in Sausalito. Art stayed independent. Art stayed in the studio named after universal love, making clay move with his hands, telling stories about a green figure who could walk into books and change shape without losing himself.

He held. For more than fifty years, he held.

Through the original Gumby Show on NBC in the 1950s. Through the lean years when the culture moved on to other things. Through the revival in the 1980s, when Art directed ninety-nine new episodes of Gumby Adventures in his sixties, still making clay move, still telling stories. Those episodes were top-rated on Nickelodeon for three years – proving that the audience was there, that the demand was real, that the green figure still carried the code that children instinctively recognized. Through the merchandising boom when Gumby became a pop culture icon and the offers to sell must have been considerable. Through the Gumby Movie in 1992, which no major distributor would touch for three years – not Paramount, not Disney, not Warner Bros., not any of them – until a tiny company called Arrow Releasing put it in twenty-one theaters, where it grossed fifty-seven thousand dollars and then sold a million copies on home video. A million families bought it when they could find it. The audience existed. The distribution did not.

Through all of it, he held. He held the way a root holds the soil – not by grasping, but by going deeper. He did not fight the industry. He did not protest the system. He did not write manifestos or launch campaigns or make himself a symbol of resistance. He simply continued to create, on his own terms, from his own studio, funded by his own vision, aligned with the word he had chosen. Prema. He was not a revolutionary. He was a gardener. And the garden, unlike the battlefield, does not produce the conditions for the next war. The garden produces the conditions for more life.

The Green Knight does not fight the court. The Green Knight tests the court. And if the court fails the test, the Green Knight does not burn Camelot to the ground. He simply reveals – by his presence, by his persistence, by his refusal to play by the court’s rules – that the court’s integrity was always thinner than it claimed.

Art Clokey revealed, by the shape of his life, that the children’s entertainment industry has no integrity. Not because every person in it is corrupt – most are not, most are earnest people who believe they are making good things for children – but because the structure does not reward integrity. The structure rewards sale. The structure rewards conversion. The structure rewards the transformation of the living thing into the dead product. And a man who refuses to convert – who holds the living thing alive for fifty-seven years, who dies at eighty-eight still owning what he created, still aligned with the word he spoke at the beginning – that man is a standing rebuke to the entire architecture. His life is the test. And the industry failed it.

The Lockout

I need to tell this part plainly, because it matters for understanding everything that came after.

Art completed the Gumby Movie in 1992. It was a full-length film, the culmination of decades of work, the proof that Gumby could carry a feature. And no major distributor would touch it. Not for one year. Not for two. For three years, the film sat waiting while the gatekeepers of American media – the six companies that controlled three-quarters of everything the country saw and heard – declined to distribute a movie featuring one of the most recognized characters in American culture.

This was not a market failure. In a free market, a product with a million buyers finds distribution. The VHS sales proved the audience existed. This was a structural failure – or more precisely, a structural success, because the structure was working exactly as designed. The FCC licensing system ensured that no one could reach an audience without government permission. The Telecommunications Act of 1996 ensured that the companies holding those permissions consolidated until there were only six doors to the American audience. And the people holding the keys to those doors – the executives who decided what American children were allowed to see – had their own reasons for keeping an independent, spiritually grounded, uncompromisable creator on the outside.

The same year Art’s movie was finally released through twenty-one theaters, Toy Story launched and CGI replaced claymation as the industry standard. This provided convenient cover – the narrative became that Gumby “couldn’t compete” with computer animation, that clay was quaint, that the medium had moved on. But the distribution blockade preceded the CGI shift. The movie was finished in 1992 and sat on a shelf for three years. That was not a technology problem. That was a gatekeeping decision made by specific people at specific companies who had already decided that an independent, uncompromisable creator would not be given access.

Art endured the lockout the way he endured everything – by continuing to work. He did not rage against the machine. He did not organize a protest movement. He continued to make clay move. He continued to tell stories. He continued to operate Premavision from Sausalito, running his own studio, holding his own rights, speaking his own word into the clay. The lockout proved that the system would not accommodate him. It did not prove that he needed the system. The distinction matters. The sword denied him access. The seed did not need access. The seed needed only time.

What He Was Not

I do not want to turn my grandfather into a saint. Saints are useful for worship and useless for understanding. Art Clokey was a human being – eccentric, stubborn, driven by a vision that the world often found impractical and the industry found incomprehensible. He was not a strategic genius who saw the future of media and positioned himself accordingly. He was a man who believed something and refused to stop believing it, and his refusal happened to place him on the right side of a principle that has been operating since before humanity learned to write it down.

He was not a businessman. He could have been wealthier. He could have licensed Gumby sooner, distributed more aggressively, played the industry game with more sophistication. He chose not to – and I honor the choice, but I do not pretend it was strategically optimal. It was morally optimal. It was spiritually optimal. It was the choice of a man who valued the soul of the thing over the market value of the thing. That is a different kind of optimization, and it produces a different kind of result.

He was not a perfect father, not a perfect husband, not a man without fault. He was a man on a search – and the search consumed him in the way that searches consume the people who take them seriously. The search came first. The clay came second. The world came third. This ordering produces great art and imperfect relationships, and both are part of the record.

I say this because the temptation, when writing about a grandfather you know primarily through legacy and cultural impact, is to polish the memory until it gleams. I will not do that. I will not turn Art Clokey into a monument. He was a seed. Seeds are not monuments. Seeds are living things that carry code, and the code is what matters – not the casing, not the surface, not the polished version of the story, but the actual information encoded inside the life. The information Art carried was Prema. Universal love as a structural principle. That information survived him. That information is still expressing. That is enough. That is more than enough. That is everything.

January 8, 2010

Art Clokey died on January 8, 2010, at his home in Clovis, California. He was eighty-eight years old.

He died owning Gumby. He died owning Premavision. He died with the rights intact, the vision uncompromised, the word still spoken. More than fifty years of creation, and not a single frame sold to someone who did not understand what it meant. Not a single character handed to a corporation that would strip the love from the architecture and replace it with a revenue strategy.

I was five years old. I do not remember his death the way my father remembered it – I was too young to understand what had been lost. But I understand now. I understand that what died on January 8, 2010, was not just a man. It was a proof of concept. It was the living evidence that a human being could hold something pure for an entire lifetime without selling it. That the seed-over-sword principle was not just philosophy. It was possible. One man had done it. One man had proved it could be done.

The orphan boy who was abandoned at nine, adopted by a composer, educated by a visionary, transformed by love – that boy grew into a man who built a world of imagination, named it after universal love, held it for six decades against every pressure the most powerful entertainment industry in human history could exert, and left it to his family with the soul intact. He did not just create a character. He proved that integrity is not a luxury. He proved that you can hold something real in a world that does everything in its power to make you let go.

The Green Knight tested the court and the court failed. But the Green Knight was never defeated. He rode back out the way he rode in – green, alive, impossible – and the court was left to reckon with what it had been shown.

Art Clokey was the green figure in a world of blocks. Soft where the industry was hard. Alive where the industry was mechanical. Transformable where the industry was rigid. He walked into the story of American entertainment and became part of it without becoming of it. He entered the book without losing himself in the book. And when he left, the story was different. Not because he conquered the industry. Because he outlasted it. Because he was still standing, still green, still holding the clay, still speaking the word, long after the industry had moved on to the next trend and the next acquisition and the next quarterly report.

The seed does not fight the sword. The seed outlasts the sword. Art Clokey outlasted them all. He held the seed for more than fifty years, and when he set it down, it was not because the sword took it from him. It was because eighty-eight years of life had run their course, and the seed – the code, the Prema, the philosophy of transformation and love – passed to the next generation the way seeds always pass: not by being sold, but by being planted.

What happened after – the eight years of fighting, the deals that collapsed, the death of the tender, the sale, the stripping of the clay – that is the next chapter’s story. This chapter is about the man who planted. And the planting was true. And the code survived. And the Green Knight, even now, even after the court has been emptied and the armor has been sold for parts, is still standing somewhere in the forest, green from head to foot, waiting to see if anyone will accept the challenge.

Art Clokey. Born Arthur Charles Farrington. Orphaned at nine. Adopted by a composer. Educated by a visionary. Transformed by love. Green Knight of children’s entertainment.

He held the seed for sixty years and never let the sword touch it.

Premavision.

The vision of universal love.

The root that holds.

Chapter 17: Joe and the Storm

My Father

I need to tell you about my father.

Not the facts first – though the facts matter and I will give them to you. Not the timeline, the career summary, the list of accomplishments that people read at memorials and nod at without feeling anything. I need to tell you about the man. The actual human being who raised me. The one who drove me to school and picked me up from practice and sat at the kitchen table explaining things I did not yet have the capacity to understand. The one who believed – with a conviction I have only begun to appreciate in the years since he left – that the world could be made better by one man holding the line.

Joe Clokey. Born November 28, 1961. He studied at Cal Poly San Luis Obispo – not film school, not business school, but horticulture. The science of growing things. I believe he chose horticulture because film and business were things he already knew – he grew up inside them. He was raised by Art Clokey. The set was his childhood. The industry was his inheritance. He did not need a degree to understand media or commerce – those were the means to the end, not the end itself. He needed the living system that gave the means their purpose. So he studied how things grow. He never told me this. But I understood it once I started doing the same thing – studying bioinformatics instead of film or business, because the technology and the entrepreneurship were already in my hands, and what I needed was the living system to apply them to. The pattern repeated before I recognized it.

I think about this constantly now, because everything he did for the rest of his life was an expression of that training. Not metaphorically. Structurally. Horticulture is the patient, disciplined, season-dependent work of cultivating something alive. You study the soil. You learn the conditions. You plant at the right time and you wait, and the waiting is not passive – the waiting is the work, because the gardener’s job is not to force the plant to grow but to protect the conditions under which the plant grows itself. My father studied how things grow. He spent his life trying to grow something in soil that had been poisoned against it.

He founded San Luis Video Publishing and produced over two hundred educational films. Two hundred. Not blockbusters. Not viral content. Not the kind of work that makes a man famous or wealthy or the subject of profiles in industry magazines. Educational films – the quiet, necessary work of taking knowledge and making it accessible to people who need it. The kind of work that does not appear on any list of cultural influence but makes the world slightly more intelligent, one viewer at a time, the way a gardener makes the world slightly more alive one plant at a time.

He married my mom, Jora Landis. They had three children: Shasta, Sequoia, and me. Sage. The youngest. Named by my brother Shasta, like my siblings, for something that grows. A mountain, a tree, and a plant. Earth, endurance, and wisdom. Our names were not an accident. Our father studied horticulture. He named his children for the things he spent his life learning to cultivate.

He did not seek attention or platform or influence for its own sake. He was a man who worked. Who showed up. Who kept his word. Who believed that the right thing, done consistently over a long enough period of time, would eventually prevail – that the system, for all its flaws, would ultimately recognize something real when it was placed before it with enough patience and enough integrity.

He was wrong about that last part. At least in the way the world measures rightness. But I am getting ahead of the story.

The Inheritance

When Art Clokey died on January 8, 2010, the world lost a creator. Our family lost the man who had held Gumby for more than fifty years. And my father inherited a mission he did not choose and could not refuse.

Joe became president of Premavision – Clokey Productions. The studio Art had named after universal love. The company that had never been sold, never been acquired, never been handed to a conglomerate for distribution or a corporation for monetization. The thing that was still alive, still carrying the code, still encoded with Prema.

But alive is not the same as safe. Alive is not the same as funded. Alive is not the same as understood by an industry that had spent the decades since Art’s heyday consolidating into six companies that controlled three-quarters of everything Americans saw and heard. The world Art planted into and the world Joe inherited were not the same world. In Art’s era, an independent creator could still reach an audience. The channels were fewer, but the gatekeepers were less concentrated. An independent studio in Sausalito could produce content and find distribution without surrendering the seed. It was never easy. But it was possible.

By the time Joe took the reins, the channels had narrowed to the point of closure. The Telecommunications Act of 1996 had removed the caps on media ownership while keeping the government licensing structure intact. Fifty media companies became six. The gatekeepers did not just control access – they controlled the terms of access. And the terms were always the same: surrender the intellectual property, or we do not distribute. Hand us the seed, or we will not let you plant it.

My father did not inherit a business. He inherited a seed in a world of swords. And he spent the next eight years of his life – the last eight years of his life – trying to plant it in soil that refused to receive it.

The Eight Years

I am going to tell this part plainly, because it deserves plain telling. The facts are heavy enough without decoration.

Classic Media: Six Years of Nothing

Joe’s first major partnership was with Classic Media. In October 2006, they announced a long-term joint venture. Classic Media would manage US and international television, film, home video, digital, and promotional rights. Tom Sarnoff – the former West Coast executive vice president of NBC, the same man who had originally greenlit The Gumby Show on NBC in 1956 – negotiated the deal for the Clokey side. The plan was a direct-to-video movie targeted for 2008 and an all-new television series, with Clokey Productions producing.

Six years. He worked with them for six years. He attended meetings. He flew to offices. He pitched concepts. He waited for calls that came late or did not come at all. He followed up on promises that evaporated on contact. He trusted the process, because he was a man who trusted processes – who believed that if you showed up with something real, the system would eventually recognize it.

Nothing was produced. Not a single episode. Not a single film. Not a single minute of new Gumby content. Six years of a man’s life spent in the machinery of an industry that had no intention of using the machine to create anything. The machinery existed to hold the property – to sit on the rights, to occupy the space, to prevent anyone else from using the brand while deciding whether it could be monetized on the industry’s terms. When the license expired on September 30, 2012, the rights reverted to Premavision. Joe had kept ownership. But six years of potential production had been consumed. Six years of a man’s finite life force had been burned in a machine that was never switched on.

In July 2012, DreamWorks Animation bought Classic Media for $155 million. Later, NBCUniversal bought DreamWorks. The company that had held the Gumby license for six years and done nothing with it was absorbed into the corporate stack. The pattern was already visible to anyone looking: touch the property, produce nothing, absorb or be absorbed. Classic Media also bought VeggieTales at a bankruptcy auction for $19 million – another beloved independent children’s property, another creator who lost everything. They touched both properties. They produced nothing with either one.

The Jim Henson Company

After Classic Media, Joe pursued the Jim Henson Company. This should have been the natural partnership – Henson’s legacy of handmade characters, of believing that craft could carry story, of understanding that the physical texture of a puppet or a clay figure is not a limitation but a language. Joe believed the Henson company would understand what Gumby was. That they would see the clay, the Prema, the living quality of stop-motion animation, and say: yes, this is our tradition too. Let us do this together.

In April 2015, the Jim Henson Company and Premavision announced a new full stop-motion Gumby series targeting fall 2016 delivery. This was the most faithful attempt to honor Art’s vision – classic Plasticine claymation, updated designs, stories about friendship and courage. It was real. It was designed. It was ready.

It needed a co-production partner. In the language of the industry, this means someone else had to put up the money. And no one would. Not a single company in the entire children’s entertainment landscape would fund a stop-motion Gumby series in partnership with Jim Henson. One of the most recognized characters in American culture, paired with one of the most respected names in children’s entertainment, and no one would write the check.

By 2015, six companies controlled virtually all children’s entertainment funding and distribution. An independent co-production partner with the resources to fund a children’s series essentially did not exist anymore. The consolidation had been so complete that there was no one left outside the system with the capacity to say yes. The co-production partner never materialized. The project died in silence. No follow-up announcement was ever made.

Amazon

Then Amazon. The new player. The disruptor. The company that was supposed to be different – that had the resources, the platform, the appetite for content that the traditional studios lacked. Joe pitched Gumby to Amazon.

It fell through. I do not know all the reasons. I was young. The details were kept from me, the way parents keep the weight of their failures from their children – not out of dishonesty, but out of love. Out of the belief that a child should not have to carry what the parent is carrying.

It fell through.

Papercutz Comics

A Gumby comic series launched in July 2017 through Papercutz. Three issues. Three. After six years with Classic Media, after the Henson dead end, after Amazon – three comic books, and then cancelled. Three issues is not a market test. Three issues is not enough time to build an audience. Three issues is the industry giving you just enough rope to believe the door is open before closing it again.

Gumby Imagined

In November 2017, three months before he died, Joe and my stepmother Joan published Gumby Imagined – a beautiful book documenting the art, the history, the creative process behind Gumby. It was an act of love. It was also an act of record. A way of ensuring that the why behind the clay was written down, captured, preserved in a form the industry could not block – because a book does not need a co-production partner, does not need a network’s permission, does not need a gatekeeper’s approval.

I think, looking back, that they knew. Not consciously. Not in a way they would have articulated at the dinner table. But somewhere in the act of writing that book – of gathering the art, telling the stories, documenting the philosophy – they were building an ark. They were putting the seed in a vessel that could survive the flood they felt coming but could not name.

The book was published in November 2017. Joe died four months later.

The Weight

I want you to understand what eight years of this does to a person. Not abstractly. Not as a case study or a cautionary tale. Concretely. Physically. In the body.

Eight years of carrying a legacy you did not choose but cannot set down. Eight years of waking every morning with the weight of your father’s life’s work on your shoulders and the knowledge that you are the only person standing between that work and the people who will strip it for parts. Eight years of walking into rooms where the people on the other side of the table smile and nod and express enthusiasm and then – nothing. A call that never comes. An email that goes unanswered. A deal that was alive last month and is dead this month and no one will tell you why.

The industry never says no. That is the cruelest part. The industry says: we’re still in discussions. The timing isn’t right. We need a co-production partner. Let’s revisit this next quarter. The industry says maybe for six years, because maybe costs nothing and no provokes a response. Maybe keeps the man on the other end of the string burning his life force in hope while the people holding the string have already made their decision and simply have not communicated it. Maybe is the sword’s most patient weapon. It does not swing. It waits. It lets gravity and time and the finite energy of a human heart do the work.

My father was not a naive man. He understood the industry. He had produced over two hundred films. He knew how the machine worked – the meetings that led to meetings that led to nothing, the enthusiasm that evaporated between Tuesday’s phone call and Friday’s silence. But he believed – and this was his greatness and his tragedy – that the machine could be persuaded. That if you brought something real, something alive, something carrying more than fifty years of unbroken creative vision, the machine would eventually recognize it and say yes.

The machine does not say yes to living things. The machine says yes to products. And Gumby, as long as a Clokey was holding it, was not a product. It was a living thing. It had Prema in its code and a man standing over it who would not let the code be stripped out. The machine could not use it on the machine’s terms. So the machine said maybe until the man carrying it collapsed.

The Last Conversation

A few months before he died, my father said something to me that I have carried every day since.

I was fourteen years old. I did not understand what he was telling me. I have spent every year since trying to understand it, and I am not sure I am finished.

He told me he was being affected by them the same way Jim Henson was.

Jim Henson – creator of the Muppets, another independent children’s creator operating outside the corporate system – died on May 16, 1990, at the age of fifty-three. Sudden infection. Organ failure. Dead within forty-eight hours of being admitted to the hospital. He had been in negotiations to sell the Muppets to Disney – negotiations that had dragged on, that had consumed his energy, that had placed him in the position of defending his creation’s integrity against a corporation that wanted the characters without the philosophy. The deal collapsed after his death. It took fourteen years for Disney to finally acquire the Muppets.

My father saw the parallel. He saw it clearly enough to say it out loud to his fourteen-year-old son. He saw that the industry was doing to him what it had done to Henson – two independent creators of beloved children’s properties, both operating outside the corporate system, both blocked by the same gatekept structure, both wearing down under the accumulated weight of a fight the system was designed to win by attrition. He connected his own experience to Henson’s. He identified a “them.” He named the pattern.

I do not know if he told anyone else. I do not know if he meant it as a warning, a confession, a plea for someone to hear what he was enduring, or simply the truth spoken aloud because it needed to be spoken and his son was the one in the room. I know that he said it. I know that I heard it. I know that the words passed through me the way light passes through glass – I felt the warmth but could not see the shape. I was fourteen. I did not have the frame to hold what he was giving me. The frame came later. The frame is being built now.

He was telling me that the fight was killing him. That the industry he was trying to reach was unreachable – not because it lacked the capacity but because it lacked the will, and the will was absent because the people who held the gates had their own reasons for keeping an independent, principled, uncompromisable family on the outside. He was telling me that the gap between what he carried and what the system could accept was the gap that was destroying him.

February 2, 2018

My father returned from a trip to Atlanta. He collapsed. Brain bleed.

One month. From onset to death, one month. February 2 to March 2, 2018.

The public reports said cardiac arrest. The family knows the fuller truth: it was a brain bleed that led to cardiac arrest. The brain bleed was the initiating event. The heart stopped because the brain was already failing. The man did not die of a weak heart. The man died of something that ruptured inside his head, and the heart followed because the heart follows the brain the way the body follows the spirit.

He was fifty-six years old.

Fifty-six. Art lived to eighty-eight. Joe made it to fifty-six. The thirty-two years between those numbers – the decades of life that should have been, the conversations that should have happened, the guidance I should have received, the book he should have written, the Gumby series he should have produced – that gap is something I carry in my body, not my mind. My mind can process the fact. My body holds the weight of it. The weight is in my chest when I wake up and in my shoulders when I sit down to write and in my hands when I type these words that he should be alive to read.

He died in the middle of the fight. Not at the end – there was no end. The fight was ongoing. The calls were still being made. The pitches were still being prepared. He died the way soldiers die in wars that have no front line – not in a decisive battle but in the accumulated exhaustion of a conflict that never pauses, never declares a truce, never gives the man in the field a single day when he does not have to carry the weight.

Jim Henson died at fifty-three. Joe died at fifty-six. Two independent children’s creators. Both blocked by the same system. Both dead suddenly in their fifties. Both followed by the acquisition of their properties by the corporate structure they had spent their lives resisting. The parallel my father identified months before his death held with the precision of a pattern observed, not a coincidence imagined.
