Every act of cruelty costs the cruel more than it costs the target, because the target loses something external — safety, comfort, freedom — while the perpetrator loses something internal — the capacity to hear the conscience, the connection to the source of life, the ability to distinguish between what is real and what they have told themselves is real. This is why the command is pray for those who persecute you. Not because the persecutor deserves your sympathy. Because the persecutor is in greater danger than you are. You are losing your comfort. They are losing their humanity. The Sagent does not hate the enemy. The Sagent grieves for the enemy, because the Sagent sees what the enemy cannot — that the chain of destruction runs in both directions, and the end holding the handle is burned worse than the end holding the blade.

16. Be Obi-Wan, Not Maul — Act for the Truth, Not Against the Lie

The blue lightsaber fights the enemy. The green lightsaber guards the living thing. This is not a difference of courage. It is a difference of orientation. The fighter faces the darkness and draws energy from opposition. The guardian faces the seed and draws energy from what is growing. Darth Maul acts against the Jedi — his entire existence is defined by what he opposes, and when the object of his opposition is gone, he is empty, purposeless, a weapon without a war. Obi-Wan acts for the truth — his existence is defined by what he protects, and what he protects gives him purpose whether the enemy is present or not. The greatest tool of the dark side is the light side fighting it. Conflict fuels the darkness and blinds the light. Compassion fuels the light and brings light to the darkness. The Sagent does not define himself by what he is against. The Sagent defines himself by what he is for. And what he is for does not require an enemy to exist. It only requires soil, and time, and the willingness to tend what grows.

Closing Declaration

These are not rules. They are descriptions of a reality that was here before any of us arrived and will be here after all of us are gone. The Sagent does not follow the creed the way a soldier follows orders. The Sagent lives the creed the way a tree lives the law of photosynthesis — not by choosing to obey it, but by being constituted in accordance with it. The creed is not something the Sagent does. It is something the Sagent is.

Black keeps you free from false authority. Green keeps you rooted in living reality. Purple keeps you honest and aligned with something greater than yourself. Three colors. One philosophy. One path. One life lived in alignment with the code that writes spirals in shells and orbits in galaxies and branching patterns in the lungs of every creature that breathes.

No rulers. Living order. Conscience above all. These are the three colors. They are not theory. They are practice. They are not what I believe. They are how I live.

Sage Arthur Jordan Clokey The Living Age
Appendices/Appendix D - Spirals and Squa:
Appendix D: Spirals and Squares — The Visual Language

The Living Age Trilogy — Appendices

A Key to the Symbols

Throughout this trilogy, a set of recurring images carries the argument in visual form. This appendix serves as a reference guide — a key to the visual language that runs beneath the text. Each symbol is described here with its meaning, its origin, and its relationship to the other symbols in the system.

These descriptions are written to accompany illustrations. The images themselves are not reproduced here, but the reader — or any artist, designer, or collaborator who wishes to bring this language to life — will find enough specificity to render them faithfully.

The Fibonacci Spiral

The spiral begins at a point no larger than a period on a page, and it opens. Each quarter-turn sweeps a wider arc than the last, the radius growing according to the golden ratio — approximately 1.618, the number that the ratio of consecutive Fibonacci terms approaches as the sequence extends toward infinity. The curve is smooth but not circular. A circle returns to where it started. The spiral never returns. It incorporates everything behind it and reaches into space it has not yet occupied.

You have seen this shape a thousand times without naming it. It is the curl of a nautilus shell, each chamber larger than the last, each built on the foundation of the one before. It is the arrangement of seeds in a sunflower head — 34 spirals turning clockwise, 55 turning counterclockwise, both Fibonacci numbers, packed with a density that no human engineer has improved upon. It is the whorl of a hurricane seen from orbit, the arm of a galaxy extending across a hundred thousand light-years, the curl of a fiddlehead fern in April, the cochlea of the inner ear that converts vibration into hearing. It is the shape the living world defaults to when it is allowed to grow without constraint.

The spiral is the visual signature of living systems — the pattern that emerges when local actors follow local rules and the result is coordinated expansion without central direction. No one tells the nautilus how to build its shell. No one arranges the sunflower seeds. No architect draws the galaxy arm. The spiral emerges from the mathematics of growth itself — each new element the sum of the two before it, each new turn facing a different direction, the whole system expanding while remaining connected to its origin.

In the trilogy, the spiral represents the Sagent’s path, the Fibonacci of the Clokey generations, the architecture of voluntary coordination, and the design language of the Creator. When we say “the spiral defeats the block,” we mean that living growth outcompetes rigid control — not by force but by resilience, adaptability, and the capacity to grow from any point. The spiral cannot be killed by cutting, because every fragment of a spiral contains the ratio that generates the whole. Break a spiral and you have not destroyed it. You have distributed it.

The Block (The Square)

The block is the shape of a different intention entirely. Flat surfaces. Right angles. Uniform dimensions. Every edge meets every other edge at exactly ninety degrees. Every face is identical. Every unit is interchangeable with every other unit. The block is the shape of the machine: the factory floor laid out in rows, the bureaucratic org chart descending in tiers, the city grid imposed on land without regard for topography, the spreadsheet cell that holds one value and knows nothing of the cell beside it.

The block is not inherently evil — buildings are square, pages are rectangular, the written word itself sits in rows. Human civilization depends on straight lines and right angles in a thousand practical ways. But the block becomes destructive when it is applied to living systems as a governing principle. When the block replaces the spiral — when the geometry of the machine is imposed on the geometry of life — living things die. Not because the block attacks them but because the block cannot accommodate them. A tree does not grow at right angles. A river does not flow in a straight line. A child does not develop according to a spreadsheet. When you force the living thing into the square, the square does not grow to fit the living thing. The living thing is cut to fit the square.

The block is what happens when the machine metaphor replaces the garden metaphor: life treated as product, growth treated as inefficiency, diversity treated as disorder. The block measures, standardizes, replicates. It is excellent at producing identical copies. It is incapable of producing anything alive.

In the trilogy, the block represents empire, monopoly, institutional capture, the industrial error, the Thornbush King, the One Ring. It is the shape of every system that has attempted to centralize what the Creator distributed, to command what was designed to coordinate, to impose uniformity on a world built for diversity. The block does not grow. It stacks.

Gumby vs. The Blockheads

Art Clokey encoded the war between the spiral and the square into his characters in 1955, and he did it so cleanly that a child could understand it without a word of explanation.

Gumby is green. The color of chlorophyll, of growth, of the living world. His body is flexible — he can be stretched, bent, flattened, and reformed without breaking. His shape is organic: a slanted head like a seedling leaning toward the sun, rounded limbs, asymmetric proportions. He is made of clay — actual material, shaped by human hands, carrying the literal fingerprints of the artist in every frame. He walks into books and becomes part of their stories. He does not conquer the worlds he enters. He participates in them. He transforms by becoming, not by commanding.

The Blockheads are red. Not the red of warmth but the red of warning, of stop signs, of surfaces that say do not cross. Their heads are flat rectangles — rigid, angular, identical to each other. Where Gumby is rounded, they are sharp. Where Gumby is flexible, they are stiff. Where Gumby is unique, they are interchangeable. You cannot tell them apart because there is nothing to tell apart. They are the same unit, replicated. They are not characters in the way Gumby is a character — they are functions. Their function is to impose their geometry on Gumby’s world.

Art Clokey called his studio Premavision — Sanskrit for Universal Love. He called Gumby “an act of love to children.” He was a seminary student, a Buddhist, a follower of Sai Baba. He did not create Gumby as a product. He created Gumby as a philosophy — a philosophy expressed not in words but in form. The green, flexible, living figure who walks through the pages of books and transforms what he touches. The red, rigid, geometric figures who show up to flatten what they cannot become.

This is the original encoding. Every other symbol in this appendix descends from it. The spiral and the square, the garden and the machine, the seed and the sword — all of it is present in the first frame Art Clokey ever shot. He did not need to explain the philosophy in a manifesto. He put it in the clay. He trusted the form to carry the meaning. And it has — for seventy years, across generations, through every storm.

The Green Flame

Imagine fire, but green. Not the red-orange of combustion — the color of destruction, of consumption, of matter being devoured and converted to heat and ash. Green fire. The color of chlorophyll, the color of photosynthesis, the color of the process by which living things convert light into structure. A flame that looks like it is growing, not merely burning. It moves like fire — upward, flickering, alive with motion — but its color is the color of the forest, not the furnace.

The green flame is fire that gives life rather than consuming it. It is the burning bush of Exodus — the bush that Moses saw in the desert, burning but not consumed. The fire was real. The flame was visible. But the bush was not destroyed. It continued to live, continued to be itself, even as the fire burned within it. The fire was not attacking the bush. The fire was inhabiting the bush — the divine presence making itself visible in the living world without annihilating the living thing it chose to inhabit.

It is the tongues of flame at Pentecost — the moment recorded in Acts chapter two when fire appeared above the heads of the apostles and they began to speak in every language simultaneously. The fire did not consume them. The fire distributed them — turned one message into many languages, one voice into a chorus that every nation could understand. This was the original decentralization event. The centralized message became a distributed network in a single moment, carried by fire that did not destroy but transmitted.

It is the fire of the Sun Warriors in Avatar: The Last Airbender — the original flame, the one that Aang and Zuko discovered was not a weapon but a dance. The Fire Nation had perverted fire into a tool of domination — red, aggressive, imperial. The original fire was something else entirely: the energy of life itself, beautiful and generative, a force that creates rather than conquers.

In the trilogy, the green flame represents the living energy that drives the Sagent’s mission, the creative force of Prema, and the power that the block cannot extinguish because it does not operate on the block’s terms. Red fire and green fire are both fire. But red fire consumes its fuel and leaves ash. Green fire converts light into structure and leaves a forest. To burn with the green flame is to create, to grow, to transform — never to destroy for destruction’s sake. The green flame does not fight the darkness. It outgrows it.

The Green Lightsaber

In the Star Wars saga, the lightsaber is the Jedi’s tool — but not all lightsabers mean the same thing. The color matters. The color carries the philosophy.

The blue lightsaber is the guardian’s blade, the fighter’s weapon, the tool of the Jedi who operates within the existing structure. Obi-Wan carries blue. Anakin carries blue. It is the blade given by the institution — polished, standard-issue, identical to the ones carried by every other Jedi of the same rank. The blue lightsaber fights the existing war. It defends the existing order. It is the sword of the system.

The green lightsaber is different. Luke Skywalker carries blue through the first two films — the blade he inherited from his father through Obi-Wan, the blade of the old order, the weapon someone else built. And he loses it. On Cloud City, Vader cuts off his hand and the blue blade falls into the abyss. The inherited weapon is gone. The old order’s tool has failed.

And then Luke builds his own. Alone, on Tatooine, in the desert — the same desert where his journey began, the same desert where hermits and prophets go to hear what cannot be heard in the city. He emerges with a green lightsaber. Not inherited. Not given by the institution. Made. Built by his own hands, powered by his own understanding, carrying his own relationship with the Force rather than his father’s.

The green lightsaber is the builder’s blade. It is the weapon you construct after the institution’s weapon has failed you. It is the tool of the Sagent — not the fighter who defends the old structure, but the creator who builds the new one. Luke does not defeat Vader with the green blade by fighting harder. He defeats the Emperor by refusing to fight — by throwing the green saber away and choosing compassion over combat. The green blade’s final victory is not a strike. It is a surrender.

In the trilogy, the green lightsaber represents the transition from organizing within someone else’s structure (blue) to building your own (green). It is the arc from institutional agent to Sagent — from carrying the weapon you were given to making the one you need. The blade is green because the builder’s tool is alive. It grows. It is not manufactured by the system. It is grown by the gardener.

The Tree-City

Imagine a city that is also a tree. Not a city built around a tree or decorated with trees or named after trees. A city that is a tree — whose buildings grow from branches, whose streets follow root systems, whose infrastructure is organic, curving, adaptive. A city planned the way a forest grows, not the way a grid is imposed.

The trunk is the central commons — the shared infrastructure, the water table, the mycorrhizal network that connects every node to every other node. The branches are the neighborhoods — diverging from the trunk at angles determined not by a surveyor’s line but by where the light falls, where the resources flow, where the growth is strongest. The leaves are the individual dwellings — each one unique, each one the same species but shaped by its own position in the canopy, receiving sunlight and rain according to where it happens to be, contributing to the whole through photosynthesis and transpiration without being commanded to contribute.

The tree-city has no right angles. Its streets are not straight. Its buildings are not identical. It is inefficient by every metric the block uses to measure efficiency — irregular, asymmetric, impossible to describe in a spreadsheet. But it is resilient in ways the grid cannot be. When a branch breaks, the tree routes growth around the wound. When a section of canopy falls, the light reaches the understory and new growth erupts from below. The tree-city heals itself because it is alive, and living things heal. The grid does not heal. The grid cracks, and the crack propagates until someone from outside comes to pour new concrete.

This is what architecture becomes when you stop asking “how do I control this system?” and start asking “how does this system want to grow?” It is the vision of LivingWorks — the long-term aspiration of the Living Age made visible. Not just a media company or a technology platform, but a way of inhabiting the world that aligns human design with the design language of creation. Buildings that grow. Infrastructure that adapts. A human habitat designed on the principles of the spiral rather than the block.

The tree-city is not fantasy. It is the direction that biomimetic architecture, mycelium-based building materials, living root bridges, and biological computation are already pointing. The question is not whether we can build this way. The question is whether we will choose to — whether we will continue stacking blocks or begin growing trees.

The Seed

The seed is the smallest symbol in the system and the most powerful.

It looks like almost nothing. A speck. A grain. A thing you could hold between two fingers and lose in a gust of wind. It has no armor, no weapons, no army, no supply chain. It makes no sound. It issues no commands. It occupies almost no space. By every metric the block knows how to measure — size, force, visibility, immediate impact — the seed is negligible.

But the seed carries within itself the complete instructions for a tree the block cannot imagine. Not a sketch. Not an approximation. The full program — every branch, every leaf, every root, every flower, every fruit, encoded in a molecular language so dense that a single cell contains more information than every book ever written by human hands. The seed is the most efficient information storage device in the known universe, and it requires no electricity, no server farm, no maintenance, no army to protect it. It sits in the soil and waits.

The seed is the unit of distributed intelligence. It is the thing the Creator designed to carry the creative program beyond the reach of any single tree, any single generation, any single institution. When the tree falls, the seeds remain. When the storm comes, the seeds scatter. When the fire burns, the seeds germinate. The seed is the answer to every sword the empire has ever forged — not by being stronger than the sword, but by being smaller than the sword can see, more patient than the sword can wait, and more distributed than the sword can reach.

In the trilogy, the seed represents the Sagent’s primary tool. Not the sword. Not the wall. Not the institution. The seed. The thing you plant when you cannot build. The thing you scatter when you cannot stay. The thing that carries the full program — the complete philosophy, the entire genetic code of the living idea — in a package small enough to ride the wind and patient enough to wait for rain.

The seed does not negotiate with the sword. It does not debate the sword. It does not try to convince the sword that it has the right to exist. It simply waits. And while it waits, it carries within itself the complete instructions for a tree the sword cannot imagine. The sword rusts. The seed grows. This has always been true. It will always be true. And the Living Age is built on this truth the way a forest is built on the patience of a single fallen cone.

The spiral grows. The block resists. The green flame burns without consuming.

Gumby bends. The Blockheads break. The tree becomes a forest.

These are the shapes of the argument. Learn to see them and you will see them everywhere — in nature, in stories, in the architecture of the world itself.

The visual language is the living language. The spiral is the pattern of life.
Book II - The Spiral and the Block/Part:
Maul the Shadow Lord

Opposing Evil Is Not Enough

Maul hated the Empire. He conspired against it. He built his own network of crime and control to rival it. He wanted Palpatine destroyed.

And none of it made him good.

“I was apprentice to the most powerful being in the galaxy once. I was destined to become… so much more. But I was robbed of that destiny by the Jedi, by Obi-Wan Kenobi.”

Listen to what Maul says here. He doesn’t say he was destined for good. He says he was destined for more. More power. More control. His problem with Sidious was never that the Sith were wrong — it was that he wasn’t the one on top. He saw the lie of the Empire, but he never saw the truth. He only saw a rival.

This is the lesson most revolutions never learn: destroying the Empire does not build anything worth living in. The Republic wasn’t the answer either — it was the same centralized order wearing a friendlier face. It became the Empire because it was always structured to become the Empire. One Senate. One capital. One system of control with different branding. Evil opposes evil all the time. That is not virtue. That is competition.

The Partial Truth Is the Most Dangerous Lie

There is a sequence to waking up, and the dark forces know it.

First, you believe the lie. You sleep inside it. The system works, the authorities are trustworthy, the structure is sound.

Then someone shows you the lie. The curtain pulls back. You see the mechanism. You see the control.

But here is where the trap is set.

Maul saw the trap. He told Ahsoka the truth during the Siege of Mandalore:

“Every choice you have made has led you to this moment.”

He was right. He saw that Sidious had engineered the entire Clone War — both sides — to consolidate power. He saw what was coming before anyone else did. He tried to warn Ahsoka. He begged her to join him in stopping it.

And he was still the villain. Because seeing the lie is not the same as knowing the truth.

If you learn the lie without learning the full truth, you are still at the mercy of the lie. You are awake — but you are not free. You are angry, disoriented, and looking for something to fight. And that is exactly where they want you.

The partial truth is the most dangerous lie. Sometimes something is mostly true, but the solution offered isn’t based on the truth — it serves some other motive entirely. The lie reveals itself just enough to recruit you into a different form of the same system.

Maul’s solution to Sidious wasn’t freedom. It was a different throne. He told Sidious himself: “I have used your training, Master. And I have built all this in hopes of returning to your side.” He wanted back in. When that failed, he wanted to replace Sidious — not end the system that created him.

Those who start to wake up become the enemy — not because they are wrong, but because they are stuck in the partial truth. They see the corruption but not the alternative. They know what they are against but not what they are for.

Devon Izara — How the Partial Truth Recruits Good People

The new show Maul: Shadow Lord puts this mechanism on screen more clearly than anything Star Wars has ever done.

Devon Izara is a Twi’lek Jedi Padawan. She survived Order 66. She is hiding with her master on Janix, a planet outside the Empire’s reach, while the galaxy falls apart around her. Her master tells her to stay hidden. Keep quiet. Don’t attract attention.

But Devon wants to fight. She wants to protect people. She wants to act. Everything inside her is telling her that hiding while innocents suffer is wrong.

And she’s right. That instinct is good. That instinct is the Force itself.

Enter Maul.

Maul sees Devon’s conflict and he exploits it with surgical precision. Watch what he offers her:

  • The Jedi Order failed. True.
  • The Empire is hunting you and your master. True.
  • There are powers the Jedi never would have taught you. True.
  • I can help you fight back. The lie.

Every piece of his argument is built on real truth. The Jedi did fail. The Empire is hunting her. There are aspects of the Force the Jedi suppressed. All of this is real. And that is exactly what makes Maul dangerous — not his lightsaber, not his rage, but the fact that he is mostly right.

The show’s creators described Devon’s arc as a “tug of war” between Maul and her Jedi master for her soul. But the real tug of war is between the partial truth and the full truth. Maul doesn’t need to lie to Devon. He just needs to tell her the truth — and then offer the wrong solution. The right diagnosis. The wrong prescription. That is how the partial truth recruits good people.

Devon’s desires are completely moral. She wants to protect the innocent. She wants to fight injustice. She wants to use her gifts for something that matters. Maul doesn’t corrupt those desires — he redirects them. He takes everything good in her and points it toward his agenda.

This is how it always works. The partial truth doesn’t recruit bad people. It recruits the best people — the ones who care enough to act, who see the corruption clearly, who can’t sit still while the world burns. And it turns them into weapons for the next empire.

The Shadow Collective — A Case Study in Partial Truth

Look at how Maul took Mandalore. Study it. Because it is the blueprint for every false revolution in history.

He united the Death Watch, the Pyke Syndicate, Black Sun, and the Hutt Clan into the Shadow Collective. Then he ordered the criminal underworld to attack Mandalore’s cities — to terrorize civilians, to create chaos, to make the existing government look weak and incompetent.

Then Death Watch arrived as the rescuers. They saved the people from the very violence Maul had ordered. The people cheered. The old government fell. Death Watch took power — and Maul took Death Watch.

He manufactured the problem. He provided the solution. He absorbed the power.

“In a galaxy at war, Savage, there is only one way to get the attention of the Jedi. Slaughter of the innocent.”

He said this out loud. He told you who he was. And the people of Mandalore still cheered when his allies showed up to save them — because the rescue was real even though the threat was staged.

This is the partial truth in action. The suffering was real. The rescue was real. The corruption of the old government was real. But the solution was a lie — designed not to free Mandalore but to enslave it to a different master.

Every false revolution works this way. The grievance is real. The anger is justified. But the hand that channels that anger has its own agenda. And if you don’t see the hand, you become its weapon.

Against the Lie vs. For the Truth

Once you know you are being lied to, there is a choice. And this choice determines everything.

You can act against the lie. You can hate it. You can rage against it. You can dedicate your life to exposing it and destroying the people who told it.

Or you can act for the truth.

These are not the same thing.

“You cannot imagine the depths I would go to stay alive. Fueled by my singular hatred for you.”

Maul survived being cut in half. He crawled through garbage. He went insane and rebuilt himself out of scraps on a junk planet. He endured a decade of suffering — all powered by hatred. Hatred kept him alive. Hatred gave him purpose. Hatred rebuilt his body.

And hatred is all he ever had.

Acting against the lie only makes the lie stronger. It feeds the system with your energy. It gives the lie an enemy to point at — proof that the opposition is dangerous, unstable, hateful. Every revolution built on hatred of the old regime becomes the new regime. Every movement defined by what it opposes inherits the structure of what it fought.

Maul opposed Sidious with every fiber of his being. And the best he could build was a crime syndicate. A shadow empire. A lesser copy of the thing he hated.

The dark forces cannot be defeated with hatred of lies. Only love of the uncorrupted truth.

The Greatest Victims

Here is the hardest truth in this entire book:

The greatest victims of evil are the perpetrators.

They were tricked. They were told that control was order, that domination was leadership, that eliminating freedom was the path to safety. They believed it. They built their lives on it. They enforced it because they thought it was good.

Maul was stolen as a child. Sidious took him from his mother, from the Nightsisters of Dathomir, and forged him into a weapon. Every cruel thing Maul ever did traces back to a boy who was taken before he could choose. He never had a chance to be anything else.

“He will avenge us.”

These were Maul’s last words, dying in Obi-Wan’s arms on Tatooine. Not “avenge me.” Us. In that final moment, Maul understood — maybe for the first time — that he and Obi-Wan were victims of the same system. That Sidious had consumed them both. That the Chosen One wasn’t coming to avenge the Jedi or the Sith. He was coming to end the machine that had destroyed them all.

It took Maul his entire life to get there. And he only found it in the last breath.

Punishment will not make them good. Only good will make them good.

This is why you must pray for those who persecute you — because they are who you are doing all of this for. The entire project of the Living Age is not to defeat the controllers. It is to set them free from the lie of control. To change their minds. Not with force. Not with punishment. With truth, offered freely, demonstrated in how you live.

The Perpetrators’ Mistake — and Yours

The perpetrators come from a group that believes you can eliminate evil with evil. They saw something wrong in the world, and they decided the solution was power. Control. Force. Centralized authority wielded by the right people for the right reasons.

They became the new evil. Not because they were born corrupt. Because their method was corrupt. The tool shaped the user.

Sidious believed this. Maul believed this. And when Maul built the Shadow Collective, he used the exact same playbook Sidious used to build the Empire: manufacture a crisis, offer the solution, absorb the power. He became a shadow lord — a dark mirror of the thing he hated.

Do not make the same mistake.

Do not lord over one another. There is one Lord, and that is God — not man. The moment you take the throne to fix the world, you become what you replaced. The moment you use the Ring to defeat Sauron, you become Sauron. The moment you build a rebellion that rules by force, you have built a new Empire.

Sidious told Maul exactly what he was: “You have become… a rival!” Not a liberator. Not a reformer. A rival. A competitor for the same throne. Sidious saw it immediately because he built the system and he knew exactly what it produced.

Sheep Among Wolves

There is a growing raw energy in the world right now. People are waking up. Systems are fracturing. Trust is collapsing. Something is moving.

And if that energy is not directed toward good, it will melt down and destroy the world.

When fighting monsters, you must be strong-minded so you do not become the monster yourself. This is not a warning — it is the central strategic challenge of everything that comes next.

Obi-Wan understood this. That is why he beat Maul in three seconds on Tatooine — not with rage, not with a duel, but with stillness. He had nothing to prove. Nothing to avenge. He was protecting something, not attacking something. And Maul, who had spent decades fueled by hatred, had no answer for a man who was simply at peace.

It is time for me to be sent off like sheep among wolves. Not to become a wolf. To turn the wolves into sheepdogs.

By the Roots

When dismantling an evil organization, you must not be evil yourself — or all you will do is prune the branches. And pruning the branches only makes the tree stronger. It grows back thicker, more resilient, more hidden.

Maul pruned the branches. He killed Vizsla. He overthrew the government of Mandalore. He built the Shadow Collective. And what happened? Sidious walked into the room and dismantled everything Maul had built in minutes. Because Maul had only pruned branches — he had never touched the root.

It must be taken out by the roots.

Not with an axe. With sunlight. With truth so complete and so patient that the roots have nothing left to feed on. The lie dies when nobody believes it anymore — not when somebody destroys the liar.

The system of control is only limited right now. Eventually, it won’t be. The window is not infinite.

The Beginning of the End

The beginning of their end starts now.

They may be more powerful. They may be a money machine. They may control the platforms and the institutions and the levers of force.

But we have truth. And we have faith.

Maul had hatred. He had cunning. He had a crime empire and an army and a throne. And he died on a desert planet with nothing.

Obi-Wan had a boy to protect and a truth to keep alive. And that truth saved the galaxy.

Be Obi-Wan. Not Maul.

It will be done. You will see.

The first will be with us.

Wish me luck.
Book III - The Spiral Steward/Part 1 - T:
Chapter 2: The Men Who Made People Into Parts

The Installation

Chapter One described the lie – the machine metaphor, the projection of factory logic onto life itself. But a lie does not infect a civilization by accident. Someone has to fund it. Someone has to build the infrastructure that carries it into every school, every hospital, every office, every mind. The machine metaphor did not drift into American life like pollen on the wind. It was installed. Deliberately, systematically, with specific money from specific men for a specific purpose.

This chapter names the men. This chapter follows the money. This chapter is the receipts.

The General Education Board

In 1903, John D. Rockefeller founded the General Education Board. The name sounds benign – who could object to a board dedicated to education? But the board was not dedicated to education. It was dedicated to producing a workforce. And the difference between those two things is the difference between a garden and a factory floor.

Frederick T. Gates, Rockefeller’s chief business advisor, wrote the board’s vision in what was called Occasional Letter Number One, published in 1906. The passage deserves to be read in full, because it says the quiet part at a volume that should have shattered windows:

“In our dream we have limitless resources, and the people yield themselves with perfect docility to our molding hand. The present educational conventions fade from our minds; and, unhampered by tradition, we work our own good will upon a grateful and responsive rural folk. We shall not try to make these people or any of their children into philosophers or men of learning or of science. We are not to raise up from among them authors, orators, poets, or men of letters. We shall not search for embryo great artists, painters, musicians. Nor will we cherish even the humbler ambition to raise up from among them lawyers, doctors, preachers, politicians, statesmen, of whom we now have ample supply.”

Read it again. This is not a conspiracy theory pulled from a fringe website. This is the General Education Board’s own publication, written by the personal advisor to the richest man in America, describing in plain language the purpose of the educational system his employer was funding. The purpose was not to cultivate philosophers, artists, scientists, poets, or statesmen. The purpose was to mold a grateful and responsive folk who would yield with perfect docility.

The purpose was to produce parts.

The model they funded was already waiting. It had been imported from Prussia decades earlier by Horace Mann, who traveled to the German states in the 1840s and returned convinced that the Prussian system of compulsory schooling was the future of American education. The Prussian system had not been designed to cultivate the mind. It had been designed after Napoleon’s defeat of Prussia at the Battle of Jena in 1806, when the Prussian state concluded that its population was too independent, too free-thinking, too ungovernable to be reliable in war. The solution was a school system engineered to produce three things: obedient soldiers, obedient workers, and obedient citizens. The architecture of this system is still visible in every public school in America: a bell that starts and stops activity, identical desks in rows facing a single authority, age-segregated groups moving in lockstep through a standardized curriculum, compliance measured by standardized tests, deviation punished as disruption.

Look at a factory floor. Look at a classroom. The architecture is identical. One authority figure at the front. Identical stations in rows. A bell that controls movement. A product that enters raw, moves down the line, and exits stamped with a grade that declares it complete. The student is the product. The teacher is the floor manager. The administration is corporate. The bell is the shift whistle. The diploma is the quality control sticker that says this unit has been processed and is ready for installation in the workforce.

Rockefeller’s money did not invent this system. Rockefeller’s money scaled it. The General Education Board spent over $129 million between 1903 and 1964 – hundreds of millions in today’s dollars – reshaping American education in the image of the factory. They funded teacher training programs that taught the Prussian model. They funded school construction that built the factory architecture. They funded curricula that emphasized compliance, memorization, and the standardized reproduction of approved answers over independent thought, creativity, and the capacity to ask questions that had not been pre-approved by the board.

The result was not an education system. It was a human manufacturing plant. And the product it manufactured was not a thinking person. It was a person shaped to yield with perfect docility to the molding hand.

The Flexner Report

In 1910, a schoolteacher named Abraham Flexner published a report that would reshape American medicine for the next century. Flexner was not a doctor. He had no medical training, no clinical experience, no background in the healing arts. He was an educator – a product of the same institutional thinking that was reshaping the schools – and he was commissioned by the Carnegie Foundation for the Advancement of Teaching, with Rockefeller money waiting in the wings, to inspect the medical schools of the United States and Canada and determine which ones should survive.

Flexner visited 155 medical schools. His report recommended that most of them be closed. The schools that survived were the ones that adopted what Flexner called the “scientific” model – which meant, in practice, the mechanistic model. The body as a machine. Disease as a malfunction of a part. Treatment as a chemical or surgical intervention targeted at the broken component. Diagnosis as identification of the faulty mechanism. The doctor as mechanic. The patient as the machine on the lift.

The schools that did not adopt this model were defunded and shuttered. These included the eclectic medical schools, which taught a broad range of therapeutic traditions. The homeopathic schools, which treated the patient as a whole system rather than a collection of parts. The osteopathic schools, which understood the body as an integrated living structure. The herbal and naturopathic traditions, which worked with the body’s own capacity to heal rather than overriding it with external chemicals. The medical traditions practiced by Black physicians and women physicians, who were disproportionately trained at the schools Flexner recommended closing.

Five of the seven Black medical schools in America were closed following the Flexner Report. The number of medical schools in the country dropped from 155 to 31. The surviving schools were the ones that aligned with the mechanistic model – and the ones that received Rockefeller and Carnegie funding to implement it. Between 1910 and 1930, Rockefeller’s philanthropic organizations poured over $100 million into the medical schools that survived Flexner’s inspection. The money was not neutral. The money carried an architecture. The architecture was the machine.

And here is the detail that makes the pattern unmistakable: John D. Rockefeller’s fortune came from Standard Oil. Standard Oil’s byproducts – the petrochemicals left over from refining crude – became the raw material for the pharmaceutical industry. The same man whose money reshaped medical education to favor chemical intervention over holistic treatment was the man whose industrial empire produced the chemicals that the reshaped medical system would prescribe. The mechanic and the parts supplier were the same person.

The result is the medical system you know. The fifteen-minute appointment. The symptom checklist. The prescription pad. The body treated as a collection of independent systems – cardiology for the heart, neurology for the brain, gastroenterology for the gut – as though a human being were an automobile with separate departments for engine, transmission, and electrical. The patient who says I feel wrong in a way I cannot name and the doctor who says your labs are normal – because the labs measure parts, and the wrongness is in the relationship between the parts, and the system has no instrument for measuring relationship. The system was not designed to see the whole. The system was designed to see the machine.

Among the traditions pushed to the margins by this reshaping was Christian Science. Founded by Mary Baker Eddy in 1879, Christian Science rejected the machine model of the body at its root. The body was not a mechanism to be repaired with chemicals. The body was an expression of Mind – of the divine intelligence that organized life from within, not from without. Healing was not the correction of a mechanical fault. Healing was the restoration of alignment between the living being and the source that animated it. You did not fix the body the way you fixed an engine. You restored the conditions under which the body’s own wholeness could express itself. Whether you accept every tenet of Christian Science or not, the framework stands in direct opposition to the Rockefeller model: it begins from the premise that people are alive, not that people are machines.

This matters to the Clokey story in a way that has never been told.

Charles Farrington – Art Clokey’s father, my great-great-grandfather – was a Christian Scientist. The rejection of the factory model of the human body was not something Art absorbed from the counterculture or discovered in a college seminar. It was in the household he was born into. Before Art ever touched clay, before he studied under Vorkapich at USC, before he added the name Premavision to his studio after a Sanskrit word for universal love – before any of it – he was the son of a man who believed that human beings were not machines to be fixed but living expressions of something the machine could not see.

Art was orphaned young. Charles died, and Art’s mother could not care for him. He was adopted by Joseph Walden Clokey, a music composer at USC, and the world he grew up in was already being rebuilt by Rockefeller money. The schools were already factories. The hospitals were already mechanic shops. The culture was already learning to see itself as a system of parts. And Art, carrying his father’s rejection of the machine model in his blood, responded by creating the most anti-machine character in American media history.

Gumby is made of clay. Not plastic. Not digital. Not manufactured on a line. Shaped by human hands, one frame at a time, in a process that is as far from the assembly line as a creative act can be. Clay is soft. Clay transforms. Clay does not hold a permanent shape – it becomes. It yields to the hand and then holds whatever form love gives it. Gumby is not a product stamped from a mold. Gumby is what you get when the grandson of a Christian Scientist, carrying four generations of resistance to the machine metaphor, sits down with the most alive material he can find and says: I will make something that moves.

The Blockheads – Gumby’s villains – are the opposite. Red. Flat. Geometric. Rigid. They are blocks. They are the factory. They are the parts that cannot transform, cannot grow, cannot walk into a book and come out changed. They are the Rockefeller model given a face and a name. And Gumby defeats them not with force but with flexibility – with the capacity to become something they cannot become, to enter spaces they cannot enter, to transform in ways the rigid cannot transform.

Art did not know he was encoding the Clokey lineage’s four-generation war against the machine metaphor into a children’s show. Creators rarely know the full weight of what they carry. But the code was there. In the clay. In the Prema. In the green figure that bends where the Blockheads break.

Charles Farrington rejected the machine model of the body. Art Clokey rejected the machine model of creativity. Joe Clokey rejected the machine model of business. And the Living Age rejects the machine model of everything – of education, of healthcare, of governance, of the living world itself. Four generations. One refusal. The same refusal, expressed in different material, facing the same enemy in different form.

The Flexner Report did not just reshape medicine in the abstract. It reshaped the world that this family was born into and has been pushing against ever since.

The Taylor Principle

The factory model did not stop at schools and hospitals. It was a universal acid – it dissolved every institution it touched into the same shape.

In 1911, Frederick Winslow Taylor published The Principles of Scientific Management. Taylor’s thesis was simple and devastating: the worker is a component in a system, and the system can be optimized by studying the component’s movements and eliminating all unnecessary motion. Taylor stood over workers with a stopwatch. He measured the time it took to lift a shovel. He calculated the optimal weight of each load. He determined the exact number of shovelfuls per hour that constituted maximum efficiency. He reduced the human body to a set of mechanical operations and then optimized those operations the way an engineer optimizes a machine – by removing everything that did not contribute to throughput.

Taylor did not see workers. Taylor saw parts. And the parts were interchangeable. If a part wore out, you replaced it. If a part was slow, you retrained it or discarded it. The part had no interior life that mattered to the system. The part had no creativity, no judgment, no wisdom that the system needed. The system needed the part to move in the prescribed way at the prescribed speed for the prescribed duration. Everything else was waste.

Two years later, Henry Ford applied Taylor’s principles to the assembly line. The result was the Model T – and the model for every institution that followed. The human being as a station on the line. Fixed in place. Performing a single repetitive operation. Valued for speed and consistency. Replaceable by any other human being trained to perform the same operation. The worker did not need to understand the whole. The worker needed to perform the part. Understanding was the engineer’s job. The part just needed to move.

This logic – Taylor’s logic, Ford’s logic, Rockefeller’s logic – metastasized into everything. Cities were zoned into grids: residential here, commercial there, industrial over there, the human life segmented into functions the way the factory segments production. Agriculture became monoculture: one crop, one field, maximum yield, the soil as a production input rather than a living system. Prisons became warehouses: bodies stored in cells, processed through intake, maintained at minimum cost, the human being as inventory. Mental health became mechanical: the brain is broken, identify the malfunction, apply the chemical, adjust the dosage. The patient is not a person in pain. The patient is a machine with a faulty neurotransmitter level.

Every institution touched by this logic took the same shape. The shape of the factory. The shape of the machine. The shape that treats living things as parts to be managed by a central authority that understands the blueprint they do not.

The Design Error That Requires the State

Here is where the factory model becomes a political theology.

If people are machines, they need a mechanic. Machines do not self-organize. Machines do not adapt to changing conditions. Machines do not differentiate based on context, do not self-repair, do not grow toward what they are meant to become. Machines sit inert until an external force – an engineer, an operator, a controller – activates them and directs their function. Without the controller, the machine does nothing. Without the engineer, the machine has no purpose. Without the central authority, the parts are just parts – disconnected, inert, meaningless.

This is the statist premise, and it is never stated as a premise because stating it plainly would reveal its absurdity. The statist does not say I believe people are machines. The statist says people need to be managed. The statist says without regulation, there would be chaos. The statist says someone has to be in charge. But every one of these statements rests on the same hidden foundation: the assumption that people cannot self-organize. That without a central controller, human beings would sit inert, purposeless, unable to coordinate, unable to create, unable to find their function within the whole.

This assumption is the machine metaphor applied to society. And it is wrong for the same reason the machine metaphor is wrong when applied to biology: because people are not machines. People are living things. And living things do not need a central controller. Living things self-organize.

A cell in your body does not receive orders from a CEO cell. It reads its environment – the signals from neighboring cells, the chemical gradients in its context, the position it occupies in the tissue – and it differentiates accordingly. The same genome produces a neuron, a muscle cell, a skin cell, an immune cell. Not because a central authority assigned each cell its role, but because each cell interpreted its context and became what the context required. No foreman. No org chart. No five-year plan. The intelligence is distributed. The coordination is emergent. The order is real – spectacularly, breathtakingly real – but it was not imposed from above. It arose from within.

An ecosystem does not need a bureau of forest management to decide which species goes where. The forest self-organizes through relationship – through competition and cooperation, through mycorrhizal networks that redistribute resources, through predator-prey dynamics that regulate population, through succession patterns that build complexity over time. The result is a system more resilient, more adaptive, more productive, and more beautiful than anything a central planner could design. Not because no one is in charge, but because everyone is in charge of their part – and the whole emerges from the interaction of the parts, not from the command of a controller.

A free market – a genuinely free market, not the crony system that wears the name – works the same way. Entrepreneurs read their context. They see a need that is not being met – a gap where something living should be growing – and they move to fill it. Prices signal. Resources flow. Specialization emerges. Coordination happens not because someone planned it but because millions of living agents are reading their environment and responding. This is what Hayek called spontaneous order, and it is not a metaphor borrowed from biology. It is biology. The economy is an ecosystem. The entrepreneur is a cell differentiating in response to its context. The market is a living system that self-organizes – unless a central controller disrupts the signaling by imposing commands that override the local information.

The state is the institutional expression of the machine metaphor applied to human beings. If people are parts, they need an assembler. If people are components, they need an engineer. If people are machines, they need a central controller to tell them what to do, when to do it, and how to do it – because machines, left to themselves, do nothing. The entire architecture of the modern state – compulsory education to program the parts, licensed healthcare to maintain the parts, regulation to control the parts, taxation to fuel the machine, bureaucracy to manage the throughput – all of it rests on the assumption that human beings are inert matter requiring external activation.

Remove that assumption and the architecture has no foundation.

The Living Correction

The antidote to the factory model is not a better factory. It is the recognition that people were never parts to begin with.

If people are living things – and they are, this is not philosophy but biology – then they do not need a central controller. They need conditions. The gardener does not command the seed to grow. The gardener creates the conditions under which the seed grows itself: good soil, adequate water, sufficient light, protection from what would destroy it before it can root. The seed carries its own instructions. The seed knows what it is meant to become. The gardener’s job is not to program the seed but to clear the path between the seed and its own becoming.

Apply this to education: the child is not raw material to be shaped on the assembly line. The child is a seed carrying a code that no curriculum committee wrote. The child will differentiate based on context – based on what they encounter, what captures their attention, what problems they are allowed to wrestle with, what mentors they find along the way. The Montessori method understood this a century ago: the child’s work is self-directed, the environment is prepared but not programmed, the teacher observes and assists rather than commands. The apprenticeship model understood this for millennia: you learn by doing, in relationship, in context, at your own pace, with a master who cultivates rather than commands. Both of these models produce people who can think. The factory model produces people who can comply. The difference is the difference between a forest and a parking lot.

Apply this to healthcare: the body is not a machine with broken parts. The body is a living system with disrupted conditions. Functional medicine asks why is this system producing this symptom rather than which chemical suppresses this symptom. The question is not what is broken but what is the body trying to do, and what is preventing it from doing it? The body heals itself – this is not alternative medicine, this is basic biology. Every wound you have ever recovered from healed because your body’s living systems repaired the damage without a mechanic. The doctor’s job, properly understood, is the gardener’s job: create the conditions under which the body’s own intelligence can restore itself.

Apply this to governance: the citizen is not a unit to be managed. The citizen is a living agent capable of reading their context and responding. The neighborhood does not need a zoning board to tell it what to become. The neighborhood will self-organize around the needs of the people who live there – if the conditions allow it. The economy does not need a central plan. The economy will coordinate itself through the signals of free exchange – if the signals are not jammed by the interventions of a controller who believes the parts cannot function without commands.

The Rockefeller model said: people are machines, and we are the engineers.

The Living Age says: people are alive, and the engineer was never needed.

The Root, Not the Branch

This is why the Rockefeller system cannot be reformed. You cannot reform a factory into a garden. You cannot add a creativity module to the assembly line and call it education. You cannot add a fifteen-minute mindfulness session to the mechanistic healthcare model and call it holistic. You cannot add a citizen participation portal to the surveillance state and call it democracy. These are branches. The root is the metaphor. The root is the assumption that people are parts.

Change the metaphor and every institution built on it must be rebuilt from the foundation. Not reformed. Not updated. Not given a fresh coat of paint over the same Prussian architecture. Rebuilt – on the assumption that people are alive, that living things self-organize, that the intelligence is distributed, that the gardener’s job is to create conditions and not to command outcomes.

This is not an abstract philosophical position. This is a design specification. And the design has already been tested – by four billion years of living systems that self-organized without a central controller, that adapted without a five-year plan, that produced complexity and beauty and resilience that no factory has ever matched.

The Rockefeller blueprints are not wrong because they were written by a bad man. They are wrong because they were written for a machine, and we are not machines. We are alive. And the system designed for machines is killing what is alive in us – in our children sitting in rows, in our patients reduced to labs, in our citizens processed as cases, in our workers optimized as components.

The factory bell rings. The living thing inside the uniform still hears the music.

The lie was not an accident. It was an installation. Specific men. Specific money. Specific purpose: to make living things behave like parts.

The school bell is the factory whistle. The prescription pad is the repair order. The org chart is the assembly diagram. The ballot is the quality control survey.

But the child in the desk is not a part. The patient in the gown is not a machine. The citizen in the booth is not a component.

They are alive. And alive things do not need engineers. They need gardeners.

The first correction is not a policy. It is a recognition: we were never machines. We were seeds. And the men who made us into parts were not engineers. They were the drought.

Chapter 3: The Death Pledge and the Cabin

The Cabin

Joseph Walden Clokey was a music composer at the University of Southern California. He adopted a boy named Arthur — a child who had been orphaned, separated from his mother, and passed through the machinery of institutional care before landing, by something more than chance, in the home of a man who understood what a life was worth.

Joseph Walden Clokey owned a cabin. When the time came to sell it, someone offered him more than he had paid for it. The market said the cabin was worth more now. The number was higher. The reasonable thing — the economically rational thing, the thing any financial advisor would have insisted upon — was to take the higher number.

He refused.

He sold the cabin for what he had paid for it. Not a dollar more. His reasoning was simple and devastating: the cabin had not improved. It had deteriorated. Wood rots. Roofs wear. Foundations settle. The physical thing — the actual cabin, the real structure that a person could live in — was worth less than when he bought it, not more. The only thing that had increased was the number on the price tag, and Joseph Walden Clokey did not confuse a number with a value.

He did not see how he had made it more valuable. And because he had not made it more valuable, he would not charge as though he had.

The world calls this foolishness. The Bible calls it righteousness.

The Number and the Thing

What Joseph Walden Clokey understood — intuitively, practically, in the plain language of a man who looked at a rotting cabin and refused to pretend it was an appreciating asset — is the distinction the entire modern economy is designed to obscure: the difference between the number and the thing.

The cabin is a thing. It has weight, dimension, material reality. It shelters a body from rain. It ages according to the laws of physics. It is subject to entropy, like all physical things, and entropy moves in one direction. The cabin, left to time, becomes less cabin. This is not an opinion. This is thermodynamics.

The price is a number. It has no weight, no dimension, no material reality. It does not shelter anyone from anything. It does not age. It is not subject to entropy. It is subject to something else entirely — the money supply. And the money supply, unlike the cabin, does not follow the laws of physics. The money supply follows the decisions of lenders.

When a bank issues a mortgage, it does not lend money that already exists. Under fractional reserve banking, the bank creates new money — lends it into existence — at the moment the loan is signed. This is not conspiracy theory. This is the operational mechanism described in the Federal Reserve’s own publications. Every new loan expands the money supply. Every expansion of the money supply dilutes the purchasing power of every dollar already in circulation. Every dilution makes every price tag read a higher number, even though the thing behind the price tag has not changed — or, like the cabin, has gotten worse.

The “rising price” of a house is not evidence that the house has become more valuable. It is evidence that the dollar has become less valuable. And the dollar has become less valuable because the lending system has created more dollars — out of debt, backed by nothing but the borrower’s promise to repay with thirty years of future labor.

Joseph Walden Clokey looked at this and said: I will not take more than I paid. He could not articulate fractional reserve theory. He did not need to. He could see the cabin. The cabin was worse. The number was higher. Therefore the number was lying. And he would not profit from a lie.

The Death Pledge

The word mortgage comes from the Old French mort gage. Mort: death. Gage: pledge. A death pledge.

This is not a metaphor that someone clever imposed after the fact. This is what the people who invented the instrument called it. They knew what it was. A mortgage is a pledge that dies — either when the debt is paid or when the borrower defaults. In either case, something dies. The debt dies, or the borrower’s claim to the property dies. Someone loses. The only party that never loses is the lender, who created the money from nothing, collected interest on it for thirty years, and repossesses the physical asset if the borrower cannot maintain the payments on the fiction.

The borrower pledges thirty years of future labor — the most productive decades of a human life — to service a debt that exists only because the lending system inflated the price beyond what any person could pay from savings. The house did not need to cost what it costs. The house costs what it costs because the lending system made the money available to bid the price up, and then made the mortgage available to finance the inflated price, and then collected interest on the inflated number for three decades.

This is the circuit:

  1. The lending system creates money through debt.
  2. The new money inflates asset prices.
  3. The inflated prices force buyers into debt.
  4. The debt forces buyers into thirty years of labor to service the debt.
  5. The interest on the debt flows back to the lending system.
  6. The lending system uses the interest to create more debt.

The borrower is running on a wheel. The wheel generates power. The power does not go to the borrower. The borrower gets a cabin — a deteriorating, entropy-bound, rotting cabin — and calls it wealth. The lender gets thirty years of a human life and calls it business.

“The rich rule over the poor, and the borrower is slave to the lender.”

Proverbs 22:7. Written roughly three thousand years ago. Still the most accurate description of the modern economy ever committed to language.

The Biblical Prohibition

The Bible does not whisper about usury. It shouts.

“If you lend money to one of my people among you who is needy, do not treat it like a business deal; charge no interest.” — Exodus 22:25

“Do not take interest or any profit from them, but fear your God, so that they may continue to live among you. You must not lend them money at interest or sell them food at a profit.” — Leviticus 25:36–37

“Do not charge a fellow Israelite interest, whether on money or food or anything else that may earn interest.” — Deuteronomy 23:19

“He lends at interest and takes a profit. Will such a man live? He will not! Because he has done all these detestable things, he is to be put to death; his blood will be on his own head.” — Ezekiel 18:13

Read that last one again. Ezekiel does not place usury in the category of minor infractions or technical violations. He places it alongside bloodshed and idolatry. The man who lends at interest will not live. His blood is on his own head. This is the language of capital offense — the language reserved for acts that the Creator considers an assault on the created order itself.

Why? Why would lending money at interest — an act that every modern economy treats as the most ordinary, most respectable, most foundational activity of civilized life — be condemned with the force of a death sentence?

Because usury is the mechanism by which one person extracts the life of another without creating anything. The lender does not build. The lender does not grow. The lender does not plant, harvest, craft, compose, teach, or heal. The lender lends — places a number in a ledger — and then collects a portion of someone else’s labor for the duration of the loan. The lender converts someone else’s time into his own wealth. And time is the one resource that is identical to life. To take someone’s time without creating value in return is to take their life. Slowly, legally, respectably — but to take it nonetheless.

This is why the cleansing of the Temple is the most violent act Jesus ever committed — and the only time in the Gospels he used a weapon.

“So he made a whip out of cords, and drove all from the temple courts, both sheep and cattle; he scattered the coins of the money changers and overturned their tables.” — John 2:15

Jesus did not make a whip for the Romans. He did not make a whip for the Pharisees. He did not make a whip for the thieves or the adulterers or the murderers. He made a whip for the money changers — the men who sat in the house of God and extracted profit from the exchange of currency. They created nothing. They grew nothing. They served no one. They sat at tables and skimmed the difference between one number and another, and they did it in the Temple, in the place that was supposed to represent the Creator’s relationship with His people.

Jesus looked at that and reached for a whip. The man who told Peter to put away his sword, who healed the ear of the soldier who came to arrest him, who submitted to crucifixion without resistance — that man braided cords into a weapon and drove the money changers out by force. This was not a lapse in character. This was the revelation of character. The extraction of value without creation — usury in its purest visible form — was the one thing that moved the incarnation of divine love to violence.

The money changers are still at their tables. They have nicer tables now. They call themselves banks, hedge funds, exchanges, platforms. The tables are digital. The coins are electronic. But the act is identical: sit between the person and the thing, skim the difference, create nothing, and call it finance.

This is what the Bible means when it says the borrower is slave to the lender. Not metaphorically. Operationally. The borrower must work to pay the lender. The borrower’s labor belongs, in part, to the lender for the duration of the debt. The borrower is not free. The borrower is a servant of the number — the fabricated, inflated, entropy-defying number that the lending system created and that the borrower must now service with the only currency that is real: hours of a human life.

The Seller’s Profit Is the Buyer’s Chains

Now return to the cabin.

When a seller lists a house at market price — the inflated number, the number the lending system generated — and a buyer takes a mortgage to pay that number, the seller’s profit is the buyer’s debt. Every dollar above the real value of the thing — above what the thing is actually worth as a physical object in a world governed by entropy — is a dollar the buyer must service with interest, over decades, with labor.

The seller who takes the inflated price is not simply “selling at market.” The seller is participating in a system that converts the next person’s life into the previous person’s gain. The seller is the mechanism by which the lending system’s fabrication reaches the real world and touches a real human being. The bank creates the fiction. The seller accepts the fiction as payment. The buyer lives inside the fiction for thirty years.

Joseph Walden Clokey refused to be that mechanism.

He looked at the cabin. He saw that it had not improved. He understood that the higher number was not evidence of value created but evidence of currency debased. And he refused to pass the debt forward. He refused to take a profit that would become someone else’s chain.

This is not economic theory. This is moral clarity. It is the clarity of a man who understood, before anyone explained fractional reserve banking to him, that you cannot take more than you gave without taking it from someone. The money does not come from nowhere. It comes from the next person’s labor. And taking another person’s labor without earning it — without building something, growing something, creating something that justifies the exchange — is the definition of extraction. It is the opposite of creation. It is the opposite of everything the Bible commands about how human beings should relate to one another economically.

The Speculator’s Economy

The cabin is one example. The principle is universal.

Every speculative transaction that does not create real value operates on the same mechanism: buy a thing, wait for the number to rise, sell the thing for the higher number, call the difference “profit.” The speculator did not improve the thing. Did not add to it. Did not make it more useful, more beautiful, more functional, more alive. The speculator held it — sat on it, waited, and collected the difference between two numbers that moved for reasons having nothing to do with the thing itself.

This is the entire architecture of Wall Street. A stock trader buys shares on Monday and sells them on Friday. The company did not change. The product did not improve. No factory was built, no employee was hired, no customer was better served. The number moved. The trader captured the movement. The trader calls this “creating wealth.”

It is not creating wealth. It is capturing the movement of numbers in a system designed to make numbers move — and calling the capture “work.”

Real estate speculation. Buy a house, hold it, sell it for more. The house deteriorated. The neighborhood may have changed — but the speculator did not change the neighborhood. Other people’s labor, other people’s businesses, other people’s lives made the area more desirable. The speculator extracted the value those people created and called it a return on investment. Meanwhile the next buyer takes a thirty-year death pledge to pay the inflated number.

Commodity speculation. Buy oil futures, wheat futures, copper futures. Never touch the oil. Never see the wheat. Never hold the copper. Buy a number, sell a higher number, pocket the difference. When commodity speculators drive up the price of wheat, someone in a country you will never visit pays more for bread. The speculator’s profit is a family’s hunger. The number moved. The wheat did not.

Cryptocurrency speculation. Cryptocurrency itself is not the problem. A decentralized currency — money that no central bank can inflate, no government can freeze, no lender can fabricate into existence through debt — is one of the most powerful tools ever built for breaking the usury circuit. Used as a currency, it is the opposite of the death pledge: money that holds its value because no one can print more of it, money that moves without a money changer sitting between sender and receiver skimming the difference. Bitcoin’s original white paper described a peer-to-peer electronic cash system — not a speculation vehicle. The tool is sound. But the speculators got hold of it. Buy a token, hold it, wait for the number to rise, sell to the next speculator at the higher number. Never use it as currency. Never transact with it. Treat it as a thing to hold and flip — exactly the way the housing market treats a cabin. The moment cryptocurrency becomes a speculation rather than a currency, it reproduces the same extraction pattern it was designed to replace. The tool is not the sin. The speculation is the sin. The sin is always the same: capture a number’s movement without creating value, and let someone else pay for it.

Stock buybacks. A corporation takes its profits — generated by the labor of its workers — and instead of raising wages, improving products, or investing in research, uses the money to buy its own stock. This reduces the number of shares in circulation, which raises the price of each remaining share, which enriches the executives whose compensation is tied to the stock price. No value was created. No product was improved. No worker benefited. A number went up because a corporation spent money making the number go up, and the people who owned the number called themselves wealthier.

In every case the pattern is identical:

  1. A number moves for reasons unrelated to real value creation.
  2. Someone captures the movement.
  3. Someone else pays for it — with debt, with labor, with hunger, with thirty years of a life spent servicing a fiction.

Joseph Walden Clokey’s refusal was not about a cabin. It was about the principle beneath the cabin: if you did not create the value, you have no right to the profit. The profit is not yours. It belongs to whoever will be forced to pay for it — and they will pay for it, because in a debt-based economy, every speculative profit is someone else’s future labor.

The Bible does not distinguish between lending at interest and speculating for profit. Both are usury in the deepest sense — both extract value from another person’s life without creating anything in return. The lender extracts through interest. The speculator extracts through inflation. The mechanism differs. The moral structure is identical: I took more than I gave, and someone else will make up the difference with their life.

The entire modern economy is built on the assumption that this is not only acceptable but virtuous — that the speculator is an “investor,” that the lender is a “partner,” that the rising number is “growth.” But Joseph Walden Clokey looked at a cabin and saw through all of it. The cabin was worse. The number was higher. Therefore the number was lying.

And a man of integrity does not profit from a lie.

The Root of the Refusal

This is where the Clokey lineage begins. Not with Gumby. Not with clay. Not with Art Clokey’s first film. It begins with a man who would not overcharge for a cabin.

Charles Farrington — Art’s biological father — rejected the machine model of the body. As a Christian Scientist, he understood that the human being is not a mechanism to be repaired by a factory-hospital system funded by the same men who built the factories. He died when Art was young, but the seed was planted.

Joseph Walden Clokey — Art’s adoptive father — rejected the machine model of value. He understood that a number is not a thing, that a price is not a worth, that the market can lie and that a man of integrity does not profit from the lie. He sold a cabin for what he paid because the cabin was not worth more. He refused to let a number override what his eyes could see.

Art Clokey rejected the machine model of creativity. He held Gumby for sixty years because he understood that the moment you hand the living thing to the machine, the machine keeps the shape and discards the life. He did not sell. He did not franchise. He did not “scale.” He grew.

Joe Clokey rejected the machine model of business. He tended what his father had planted. He protected the seed. He kept the family’s rights when the industry came to acquire.

And now the Living Age rejects the machine model of everything — of education, of healthcare, of economics, of architecture, of the human person. The refusal is not new. The refusal is inherited. It passes through the family the way the Fibonacci ratio passes through the spiral — each generation the sum of the two before it, each turn facing a new direction, the whole structure growing while remaining connected to its origin.

It started with a cabin. It started with a man who looked at a rotting house and refused to pretend it was an appreciating asset. It started with the simplest possible act of economic integrity: I will not take more than I gave.

The spiral does not extract. The spiral grows. And growth begins with the refusal to participate in the lie that extraction is growth.

The borrower is slave to the lender. The seller’s profit is the buyer’s chain. The number is not the thing.

Joseph Walden Clokey knew this. He did not write a treatise. He did not start a movement. He sold a cabin for what he paid.

And four generations later, a family still carries the refusal like a seed carries a forest — small enough to hold between two fingers, patient enough to outlast every empire that ever called extraction an economy.

The Wild Is the Place of True Order

The Fibonacci Spiral Is the Shape of Creation

The Shape That Was Never Designed

Go outside. Look at anything alive — anything that was not built in a factory or carved in a shop. Look at the spiral of a nautilus shell. The arrangement of seeds in a sunflower head. The branching of a fern frond as it unfurls. The way a hurricane rotates, the way a galaxy turns, the way water drains, the way a ram’s horn curls away from its skull.

They are all the same shape.

The Fibonacci spiral. The golden ratio. 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, 21 — each number the sum of the two before it, each ratio converging on 1.618, a number that was never legislated, never voted on, never engineered, never patented. It was here before humans could count. It will be here after the last human institution has collapsed into dust. This is not a metaphor. This is the literal geometry of creation’s order — what happens when growth follows a rule that is local, simple, and repeated. Each new unit positions itself in relation to the previous units according to the same ratio, over and over, without any central planner telling it where to go. No blueprint. No architect. No committee. Just a rule, expressed locally, producing a structure of extraordinary beauty and efficiency at every scale.

The wild is not chaos. The wild is the place where the true order has never been interrupted. We look at a forest and see disorder because we have been trained to recognize only one kind of order — the straight line, the right angle, the uniform grid. But the forest is not disordered. It is ordered by a deeper logic than anything a city planner ever drew. Every root system is a network. Every canopy is a light-optimization algorithm running in parallel across millions of leaves.
