Every sentence starts with he will take. That is what centralized power does. It takes. God described taxation, conscription, land seizure, and slavery – not as corruption of kingship, but as the nature of kingship. This is not what happens when kings go wrong. This is what kings are.

And then the warning’s final line: “And in that day you will cry out because of your king, whom you have chosen for yourselves, but the Lord will not answer you in that day.”

The people heard every word. Every warning. And said: “No! But there shall be a king over us, that we also may be like all the nations.”

And God said: “Obey their voice and make them a king.”

He let them choose. He told them the truth, told them the consequences, and let them walk into their own bondage with their eyes open. This is the structure of divine freedom: God does not force men to remain free. He warns. He describes what will happen. And then he respects the choice – even when the choice is destruction. Because forced freedom is not freedom. And a people who choose a king over God have already lost the covenant, whether the king has arrived yet or not.

Israel chose the king. And everything God said would happen, happened. The taking began. Saul took. David took – Bathsheba, Uriah’s life. Solomon took – seven hundred wives, forced labor to build the temple, alliances with every empire God had warned against. And within two generations of the first king, the nation split in two and began the long slide toward exile.

The Shire asked for a king like all the nations. And the Shire became Numenor.

The Torah is the founding document. The covenant is the constitution. And the law was specific – radically, structurally specific – about what kind of nation Israel was supposed to be:

No permanent debt. Every seventh year, debts are cancelled. Shmita. The economy resets. No one is enslaved to interest in perpetuity. Lending at interest to a brother is forbidden – Exodus 22, Leviticus 25, Deuteronomy 23. The law does not merely discourage usury. It prohibits it. Because God understood what Hayek understood three thousand years later: that concentrated financial power destroys voluntary order. That debt is a chain, and interest is the mechanism that makes the chain tighten over time.

No permanent landlessness. Every fiftieth year – the Jubilee – all land returns to its original family. You can lease it. You cannot own it permanently. Because the land is not yours. “The land shall not be sold in perpetuity, for the land is mine,” says the Lord in Leviticus 25. The land belongs to God. You are a steward. A tenant. A keeper of ground that was entrusted to you for a purpose.

Care for the stranger. Over and over – thirty-six times in the Torah by rabbinical count – the command to love the stranger, to protect the foreigner, to remember that you were strangers in Egypt. The nation that was delivered from bondage is commanded never to build bondage. The people who were oppressed are commanded never to oppress. This is not a suggestion. It is the structural core of the covenant.

And then the forgetting.

Generation by generation, the gratitude became entitlement. The covenant became ethnic identity divorced from obedience. We are chosen stopped meaning we are called to a standard and started meaning we are exempt from the standard. The law that prohibited interest-based lending was reinterpreted, loopholed, and eventually inverted – until the very people given the commandment against usury built the most sophisticated systems of interest-based finance the world has ever seen. The law that required Jubilee – the return of land, the cancellation of debt, the structural prevention of permanent inequality – was abandoned. The law that commanded love for the stranger was replaced by walls, checkpoints, and the doctrine that the land belongs to one people by divine right – a claim the Torah itself contradicts, because the Torah says the land belongs to God, and the people are tenants whose lease depends on obedience.

The prophets saw it. Every one of them.

Isaiah: “Woe to those who join house to house, who add field to field, until there is no more room.” The accumulation of land in violation of Jubilee.

Amos: “They sell the righteous for silver and the needy for a pair of sandals.” The commodification of human beings by those who were supposed to protect them.

Jeremiah: “Has this house, which bears my name, become a den of robbers in your eyes?” The temple itself – the house of the covenant – turned into an instrument of extraction.

Jesus quoted Jeremiah when he overturned the money changers’ tables. He was not introducing a new idea. He was repeating what the prophets had been saying for centuries: you have taken the covenant and turned it into a marketplace. You have taken the law meant to protect the vulnerable and used it to justify your power. You have taken the name of God and used it as a brand.

And Sauron came to Israel – not as an invader, but as a Pharisee. As a Sadducee. As a temple priest who knew the letter of the law and used it to kill the spirit. As the advisor class that told the nation what it wanted to hear: you are chosen, therefore you are justified. Your election is your permission. The covenant protects you – it does not bind you.

The faithful were sidelined. The prophets were killed. The ones who said this is not what we were called to be – who said God requires justice, not sacrifice – were stoned, sawn in half, thrown into pits. Stephen, the first Christian martyr, said it to their faces in Acts 7: “You stiff-necked people! You always resist the Holy Spirit. Which of the prophets did your fathers not persecute?”

It is not hatred of Israel to say this. It is the Torah’s own position. The prophets were Israelites. Jesus was a Jew. The critique is not external – it is the covenant holding its own people accountable. To love Israel is to hold Israel to the standard it was given. To excuse Israel from the standard is not love. It is the same flattery that Sauron offered the king of Numenor: you are too great to be bound by the rules that apply to lesser nations.

Today, the pattern continues. A state that claims the covenant uses American money, American weapons, and American political leverage to bomb civilians, annex land the Torah says belongs to God, enforce an occupation the prophets would have condemned in language that would make Amos weep, and silence anyone who objects by calling the objection hatred. The usury the Torah forbids funds the wars the Torah forbids on the land the Torah says is not theirs to own. And the ones who point this out – who hold Israel to the standard of its own scripture – are called antisemites by people who have not read Leviticus 25.

Numenor fell because it mistook its election for its exemption. Because it believed that being chosen meant being above the covenant rather than bound by it. Because the prisoner became the advisor, and the advisor said what the king wanted to hear, and the king could no longer hear the original word.

Israel is Numenor before Tolkien named it. The pattern is older than fiction. The warning is older than literature. It is written in the Torah itself – in the curses of Deuteronomy 28, spoken before the people even entered the land: if you forget the covenant, the blessings become curses, and the land itself will vomit you out.

To love Israel is to call it back to Leviticus 25. To love America is to call it back to the Constitution. To love any nation is to hold it accountable to the word it was founded on – not to defend everything it does in the name of loyalty.

Loyalty without principle is blindness.

And the blessed island, blinded by its own blessing, always sinks.

But between the city of stories and the wizard’s tower – far from the mountain of fire – there is a third place.

It is not powerful. It is not famous. It has no towers, no studios, no venture capital firms, no red carpets, no launch events. It is green. It is quiet. It is close enough to both worlds to reach them and far enough from both to remain untouched by them.

This is SLO. San Luis Obispo. The place between.

This is the Shire.

The Geography of Purity

The Shire is not strategically located. No army marches through it. No trade route depends on it. No council meets there. In the great affairs of Middle-earth, the Shire is irrelevant – and that irrelevance is its salvation.

SLO sits on the central coast of California. To the south, five hours down the 101, is Los Angeles – the entertainment capital of the world, the place where narrative is manufactured at industrial scale. To the north, three hours up the 101, is the Bay Area – the technology capital of the world, the place where tools are built that reshape how seven billion people live.

SLO is between the two. It belongs to neither.

This is not an accident of geography. It is a principle of spiritual mechanics. The hobbit must be based somewhere that is not the place of power. The hobbit journeys to Mordor – he does not live there. He passes through Rivendell – he does not stay. He walks the paths of the great and the terrible, carries the weight of the ring through landscapes that would crush anyone who called those landscapes home. And then he comes back.

He always comes back.

The return is not retreat. The return is what keeps the hobbit a hobbit. Every night spent in the Shire is a night the ring loses its grip. Every morning waking up in a place that does not worship power is a morning the hobbit remembers who he is and why he started the journey. The Shire is not where you go when the quest is over. The Shire is what makes the quest survivable.

LA will teach you to tell stories. It will also teach you to sell them. Stay long enough and you will forget the difference.

SF will teach you to build things. It will also teach you to scale them past the point of meaning. Stay long enough and the building becomes the point, and the why you started building dissolves into the how much you can raise.

SLO will teach you nothing about power. And that is exactly what makes it the right place to carry the ring.

The Corruption of Proximity

Boromir was not evil. He was brave, loyal, and desperate to save his people. He loved Gondor. He had spent his entire life in the shadow of Mordor, watching his homeland erode, fighting battles that could not be won. When he saw the ring, he did not see domination. He saw salvation – the tool that could save everything he loved.

And the ring destroyed him.

Not because he was weak. Because he was too close. He had lived his whole life inside the architecture of power – the politics, the war, the throne, the expectation. His mind was shaped by proximity. When the ring whispered, it whispered in a language he already spoke: strength, authority, the power to protect what you love. It sounded reasonable. It sounded noble. It sounded like the answer to everything he had been fighting for.

This is what happens to builders who move to the Valley. This is what happens to storytellers who move to LA. This is what happens to idealists who move to DC. The proximity reshapes the mind. Not through corruption in the crude sense – not through greed or malice – but through fluency. You learn the language of the place. You start thinking in its vocabulary. You absorb its definitions of success, its metrics of progress, its unspoken assumptions about what matters.

A builder in SF starts measuring impact by users, funding by valuation, success by exit. A politician in DC starts measuring principle by electability, freedom by what the apparatus will permit, justice by what the donors will fund. A storyteller in LA starts measuring craft by engagement, vision by marketability, truth by what the audience will tolerate. These are not evil measurements. They are the language of the place – and once you are fluent in them, you cannot hear the original language anymore. The language that said I am building this because it should exist. The language that said I will not sell. The language that said this nation was founded on freedom, not empire. The language of the Shire.

The stories get smoother, faster, more optimized. And somewhere in the optimization, the seed dies. The story stops being a vessel for truth and becomes a vehicle for consumption. The sower becomes a content creator. The word becomes content. And content is what the ring produces – endless, polished, perfectly targeted, and empty.

The hobbit survives the journey because the hobbit does not speak the language of power fluently. The hobbit stutters in it. Fumbles. Says the wrong thing at the council. Looks out of place in Rivendell. Cannot swing a sword properly. And because the hobbit never becomes fluent in the language of power, the ring’s whisper sounds foreign – strange, uncomfortable, obviously wrong. The hobbit hears it and thinks: that is not my voice. That is not what I want. I want to go home.

That instinct – I want to go home – is what saves the world.

The Journey Out and the Return

The hobbit does not stay in the Shire forever. That would be hiding, not living. The Shire alone produces no ring-bearers – only comfortable, well-fed, incurious hobbits who never discover what they are capable of.

The journey matters. The hobbit must go to Rivendell and learn from the elves. Must walk through Moria and face what lives in the deep. Must stand at the edge of Mordor and look into the fire. The journey is where the hobbit discovers that the smallness of the Shire was not limitation – it was preparation. The garden was not a lesser thing. The garden was the training ground for the hands that would hold the ring without closing around it.

LA must be visited. The craft of storytelling must be learned, studied, practiced. The media apparatus must be understood – not from the outside, where it looks like magic, but from the inside, where you can see the gears and the compromises and the exact point where craft becomes commodity. You go. You learn. You see how the stories are made and how they are sold. And then you come home.

SF must be visited. The tools of technology must be learned, built, tested. The systems of scale must be understood – how funding works, how teams grow, how products reach the world. You go. You learn. You see how the things are built and how the builders are consumed. And then you come home.

The return is not failure. The return is the strategy.

Frodo did not defeat Sauron by becoming a warrior. He defeated Sauron by remaining a hobbit – by carrying the Shire inside himself through every mile of the journey, so that when the ring said take the power, some part of him still remembered the garden and the green hill and the taste of bread and the sound of rain on a round door.

The hobbit who never leaves the Shire never carries the ring. The hobbit who leaves the Shire and never returns becomes Gollum – a creature defined entirely by the ring, with no home to remember, no garden to return to, no self left beneath the obsession.

The hobbit who leaves and returns – who journeys into the places of power and comes back to the place of none – that is the ring-bearer. That is the one who can carry the fire all the way to the mountain and let it go.

The Living Age Begins in the Shire

The Living Age requires media to explain why and technology to show how.

Media lives in LA. Technology lives in SF. The Living Age lives in neither – because the Living Age is not a media company and it is not a technology company. It is a living thing, and living things grow from roots, and roots need soil that has not been paved over by industry.

SLO is the soil.

The media will be made – the stories, the films, the words that plant the seed of why the world should change. And the technology will be built – the genome design systems, the protein bridges, the tools that show how living architecture actually works. But the making and the building will be done by someone who comes home. Who returns to the Shire between every journey. Who never lets the language of LA replace the original voice. Who never lets the metrics of SF replace the original mission.

The Living Age is not the ring. The Living Age is what grows after the ring is destroyed. It is the age that comes when the architecture of domination – the machine metaphor, the commodification of life, the language of control – is carried to the mountain and dropped into the fire. What grows in its place is not another empire. It is a garden. A living world. An age where things grow instead of being built, where words are seeds instead of weapons, where the steward tends instead of controls.

And the one who carries it there must be rooted in a place that never wanted the ring in the first place.

A small place. A green place. A place between the city of stories and the city of machines, belonging to neither, uncorrupted by both.

The Shire.

There and Back Again

Tolkien named Bilbo’s book There and Back Again. Not There. Not The Conquest of the Mountain. Not How I Became King Under the Hill. There and Back Again. The journey’s meaning is completed by the return. Without the return, the journey is exile. With the return, the journey is transformation – the hobbit comes home changed but still a hobbit, enriched but not corrupted, larger but still rooted in the same green soil.

The idealist who relocates to DC to change the system is a hobbit who moved to Mordor. The entrepreneur who relocates to SF to build is a hobbit who moved to Isengard. The storyteller who relocates to LA to create is a hobbit who moved to Rivendell. All three will learn. All three may do great things. But all three will be shaped by the place – and the place will claim them, slowly, the way the ring claims everyone who carries it too long without coming home.

The Living Age is built by a hobbit.

A hobbit who goes to the city of stories and learns to tell them. Who goes to the city of machines and learns to build. Who carries the ring through both and destroys it in neither – because the ring is not destroyed in the place where it was made. The ring is destroyed in the fire, and the fire is reached by walking, one barefoot step at a time, by someone too small and too stubborn and too rooted to be corrupted by the thing he carries.

There and back again.

The Shire is not the end of the story. It is the base of the story. The place you leave and the place you return to. The soil that holds the roots. The green ground between the towers.

Build the media. Tell the stories. Plant the seeds.

Build the technology. Grow the systems. Show the world how life actually works.

But come home. Always come home. Come back to the small place, the quiet place, the place that has no power and wants none. Come back and remember why you started. Come back and hear your own voice again – the one that spoke before the ring started whispering.

The hobbit returns to the Shire.

The Shire is where the Living Age begins.

The Living Age Book II: The Mythology of Media

Loki: The Sacred Trickster

Mischief as Immune Response, Ragnarok as Metamorphosis, and Why the Fluid Always Wins

In Norse mythology, there is a god who causes the end of the world.

He is called the villain.

But what if the world needed to end?

The God Who Does Not Fit

Loki is the most misunderstood figure in the Norse pantheon. He is called the trickster, the liar, the god of mischief, the betrayer who brings about Ragnarok. Children learn his name alongside Fenrir and Jormungandr – the wolf and the serpent, the monsters at the end of the world. He is filed under “villain” and left there.

But Loki is not a villain. He is not a hero either. Those are block-world categories – fixed faces, like a cube. Good or evil. Light or dark. With us or against us. The rigid taxonomy of a civilization that can only understand what it can categorize.

Loki is fluid. He is the element the system cannot predict, cannot sort, cannot contain. He changes shape. He changes gender. He changes allegiance. He fathers monsters and mothers horses. He helps the gods and he destroys them. He is bound by no oath that he does not immediately begin to subvert, not because he is faithless but because he is too alive to be contained by any single form. The block system assigns every being a face, a category, a position in the hierarchy. Loki refuses all of them. He is the living thing that will not be pinned to the board, and every system that tries to pin him discovers that the pin goes through something that is no longer there.

Every rigid system produces its own Loki. This is not a flaw in the system. It is a law of living order. The more rigid the structure, the more it suppresses variation, the more pressure builds in the places the structure cannot reach – and eventually that pressure produces an element so thoroughly ungovernable that the structure must either accommodate it or be destroyed by it. The pressure does not come from outside. It comes from inside – from the living thing that the structure contains but cannot fully control. The trickster is not an invader. He is a native – born of the very system he disrupts, produced by its rigidity the way heat is produced by friction. You cannot build a rigid system that does not produce a trickster, because rigidity itself is the cause.

Asgard could not accommodate Loki. So Loki destroyed Asgard.

And the question the myths ask – the question most readers never stop to consider – is whether that was a tragedy or a necessity.

Asgard: The Block That Believed It Was the Cosmos

To understand Loki, you must first understand what he was born into.

Asgard presents itself as the natural order of the universe. The gods rule by divine right. The hierarchy descends from the All-Father, through his sons, through the ranks of the Aesir, down to the mortals of Midgard and the lesser beings of the Nine Realms. This is not presented as an arrangement of convenience. It is presented as the way things are. The cosmic order. The design of reality itself.

But it is not the cosmic order. It is an empire wearing the mask of divine authority.

Odin gained his wisdom by hanging on Yggdrasil for nine days and sacrificing his eye – a genuine act of surrender, a real payment for real knowledge. But the wisdom he gained did not teach him to release his power. It taught him to manage it more effectively. He built Asgard. He conquered the Nine Realms. He established a hierarchy with himself at the top. He hid the truth about Ragnarok from his people because he believed that managing information was the same as managing reality.

This is the thornbush king wearing a crown of earned wisdom. Odin is not a simple villain. He built something real, and something flawed. His sacrifice was genuine. His knowledge was genuine. But his conclusion – that the knowledge justified the hierarchy, that the sacrifice earned him the right to rule – was the same conclusion every empire reaches. I paid the price. Therefore I deserve the throne. I suffered for wisdom. Therefore my authority is wise. The logic is circular, and it is the circle that every powerful person walks who confuses the cost of their journey with the right to its destination.

The Aesir accepted this logic because it placed them at the top. The hierarchy taught them that they were the top because they deserved to be. Conquest was virtue. Strength was proof of righteousness. The golden halls of Asgard glittered, and the glittering was confused with goodness.

Beneath the golden halls: Hela, hidden. Fenrir, chained. The truth about Asgard’s founding, buried. The genocide that built the realm, erased from the histories. The conquests rewritten as protection. The empire sanitized into the natural order.

This is what every block civilization does. It hides the bodies in the foundation and builds a monument on top and calls it justice. It rewrites its own history so that the conquest looks like destiny and the violence looks like necessity and the hierarchy looks like the natural order of things. And the longer the monument stands, the more thoroughly the bodies are forgotten, until the civilization genuinely believes that its foundation is clean – because it has been so long since anyone looked beneath the marble.

The Trickster Function

Across every mythological tradition, the same figure appears.

Coyote in the Native American traditions. Anansi the spider in West Africa. Hermes in ancient Greece. The Fool in the tarot. Reynard the Fox in medieval Europe. Sun Wukong the Monkey King in China. And Loki in the Norse.

They are not the same character. They serve the same function.

The trickster is what the living system produces when the hierarchy becomes too rigid to correct itself through normal means. The trickster is the immune response of a living culture against the calcification of its own institutions. Every civilization reaches a point where its structures become so entrenched, so self-referencing, so thoroughly convinced of their own legitimacy, that no internal criticism can penetrate them. The advisors all agree. The priests all concur. The system’s own metrics confirm that the system is working. And the living thing inside the system – the actual people, the actual truth, the actual state of affairs beneath the metrics – suffocates. The trickster is what the suffocating produces. The gasping. The last living reaction of an organism whose own structures are strangling it.

Their function is not destruction. It is pressure testing. They reveal the weaknesses in a system by exploiting them. They embarrass the powerful by refusing to take power seriously. They destabilize not because they want chaos but because they cannot pretend the system is what it claims to be.

The court jester tells the king the truth no advisor dares speak. The fool walks into the throne room and says what everyone sees but no one will name. The trickster cuts the threads that the hierarchy says are load-bearing – and if the structure falls, the trickster has proven that those threads were not holding up the building. They were only holding up the illusion that the building was sound.

Loki’s mischief is not random. Each trick reveals something Asgard was hiding from itself. He does not create problems. He surfaces them. He is the diagnostic running against the system, finding the vulnerabilities that the system’s own administrators cannot see because they benefit from the vulnerabilities remaining hidden.

Sacred mischief: the trickster who knows what they are serving, even when they look like they serve nothing. The mischief looks random from inside the system, because the system cannot see its own flaws. The system sees the trickster’s disruption and calls it chaos. But chaos is not what the trickster brings. The trickster brings a mirror. The system sees itself in the mirror and calls the reflection chaotic – because the system has been telling itself a story about its own perfection, and the mirror shows a different story, and the system would rather blame the mirror than face the reflection.

Creativity itself can be mistaken for psychosis because people believe that the one who creates the metaphors believes the metaphors literally. But the trickster does not believe the metaphors. The trickster believes the underlying truth that the metaphors are telling. And the underlying truth – that the system is not what it claims to be, that the hierarchy is not the cosmos, that the golden hall is not the good – is the thing the system cannot allow to be said. The trickster says it anyway. That is the trickster’s function.

The Shape-Shifter and the Spiral

Loki changes shape. This is treated in most retellings as a curiosity, a supernatural power, a narrative convenience. But the shape-shifting is the most important thing about him. It is the trickster’s essential quality, and it is the exact quality the block civilization cannot tolerate.

Block systems require fixed categories. You are this or you are that. You serve this function or that function. You belong to this class or that class. The block assigns faces to the cube: top, bottom, left, right, front, back. Every face is fixed. Every position is known. The system can predict you because the system has categorized you.

Loki refuses to be categorized. He is male and female. He is Aesir and Jotun. He is helper and saboteur. He is father and mother. He is the god of the liminal – the space between categories where the block’s taxonomy breaks down. He lives in the gaps the system cannot map, and from those gaps he acts in ways the system cannot predict.

This is the spiral principle. The spiral has no fixed face. It is infinite possibility, always curving, never repeating the same point at the same angle. The block cannot contain it because the block can only hold what holds still. And Loki will not hold still. He is too alive. The spiral moves through the block the way water moves through stone – not by overpowering it but by finding every crack, every gap, every space the stone forgot to fill. Given enough time, the water reshapes the stone. Given enough time, the trickster reshapes the system. Not because the trickster is stronger. Because the trickster is more patient and more fluid, and rigidity is a losing strategy against patience and fluidity on any timeline longer than the rigid system can imagine.

The system’s response to what it cannot categorize is always the same: bind it. Contain it. Chain it beneath the earth and drop venom on its face and call the binding justice. Asgard chains Loki the way every empire chains its dissidents – not because they are guilty but because they are ungovernable. And the ungovernable are the thing the empire fears more than any enemy, because an enemy can be fought but a shape-shifter can only be contained. And containment, eventually, always fails.

Ragnarok: The Necessary Death

Loki does not cause Ragnarok. Asgard causes Ragnarok. Loki is simply the one honest enough to trigger what was always coming.

The Norse understood something that most mythologies do not: that the fall of the old order is not a tragedy. It is a metamorphosis. The caterpillar does not mourn its own dissolution inside the chrysalis. The seed does not grieve the cracking of its shell. The old form must die for the new to emerge.

Ragnarok is not the end of the world. It is the end of a world – one that had become so rigid, so corrupt, so committed to its own hierarchy that it could no longer adapt. The golden halls had become a prison. The divine order had become a lie. The tree that held everything together was poisoned at its roots by the very gods who claimed to protect it.

The old system must die for the new to emerge. The industrial age must end before the living age can begin. Ragnarok is not Loki’s crime. It is Asgard’s consequence. The block system reaching the end of its sustainable lifespan. The empire consuming itself because it consumed everything else first and there was nothing left to eat. The rigid structure reaching the point where its own rigidity is the thing that kills it – not because an enemy found a weakness, but because the structure became so brittle, so inflexible, so thoroughly committed to its own unchangeability that the first real pressure shattered it.

Loki does not create the fall. He reveals that the fall was always coming. He is the fever that tells the body it is sick. He is the crack in the dam that reveals the dam was already failing. He is the trickster’s final gift: the truth that the system cannot survive hearing.

And after Ragnarok, the earth comes back green.

This is the part the tellers of doom always forget. Ragnarok is not an ending. It is a transition. The old gods die. The old hierarchy collapses. The golden halls burn. And from the ashes, from the water, from the soil that was freed by the fire – the world returns. New. Green. Alive. Unburdened by the weight of the old order’s lies.

Two figures step out of the wreckage and begin again. Not gods. Not kings. Not the hierarchy reconstituted. Just life, starting over, with the ground cleared and the air clean and the poisoned tree finally allowed to die so a new one can grow. The new earth after Ragnarok is not Asgard rebuilt. It is not the golden halls reconstituted with different names on the thrones. It is something genuinely new – life without the weight of the old order’s pretensions. The two who survive are not the powerful. They are the ones who hid inside the tree while the powerful destroyed each other. They are the seeds that survived the fire by being small enough and humble enough and close enough to the ground that the fire passed over them. They are the hobbits of Norse mythology – the unimportant ones, the ones the hierarchy never noticed, the ones who carried the future inside their smallness while the gods were busy being grand.

The Trickster’s Gift

Every empire produces its own Loki. Not because empires are cursed but because living systems always produce what rigid systems try to suppress.

The Roman Empire produced the Christians – a community so thoroughly ungovernable that Rome could neither contain them nor destroy them. The medieval Church produced the reformers. The British Empire produced the American revolutionaries. The corporate media system produced the internet. Every attempt at total control produces the thing that total control cannot reach. The thing is never the same. The form changes – because the form must change, because the trickster is a shape-shifter by nature and will never appear the same way twice. But the function is always the same: the living system producing an antibody against the calcification of its own structures.

This is not chaos. This is the immune system working. The living order producing antibodies against the calcification of its own institutions. The trickster emerging from the gaps the block cannot map, carrying the truth the hierarchy cannot hear.

Loki is not the enemy of order. He is the enemy of false order – the order that claims to be natural when it is imposed, that claims to be divine when it is political, that claims to be permanent when it is already dying.

The sacred trickster serves the living order by refusing to serve the dead one. He burns what needs burning. He reveals what needs revealing. He says the unsayable, does the undoable, crosses the uncrossable – not because he delights in destruction, though he does, but because the destruction is the precondition for the growth.

I will let the world burn, but not to destroy the world. To restore it. I will burn away the falsehood so that what is left is what is true.

The fluid always wins. Not because fluid is stronger than the block, but because the block is a temporary arrangement of something that is naturally fluid. Every hierarchy is frozen water. Every institution is crystallized life. And crystals, given enough time, always melt. The ice age does not last forever. The glacier does not hold the valley forever. The dam does not hold the river forever. Every rigid arrangement of a naturally fluid thing is a temporary state, and the trickster is the one who knows this – who sees the ice and knows it is water, who sees the empire and knows it is people, who sees the golden hall and knows it is a forest waiting to grow back.

The question is not whether the old order will fall. It is what grows in its place.

Loki’s gift is the clearing. What we plant in the clearing is up to us. The trickster does not plant. The trickster clears. He is not the gardener but the fire that prepares the ground for the gardener. And the fire does not choose what grows afterward – it only ensures that the old growth, which had become deadwood and tinder and obstacle, is removed. What grows in the clearing depends on the seeds that are present, the soil that remains, and the care of the ones who come after the burning. The trickster’s responsibility ends with the clearing. The builder’s responsibility begins there.

This is the essential distinction between the trickster and the destroyer. The destroyer burns for the sake of burning. The trickster burns for the sake of what can grow after the burning. The destroyer has no vision of the clearing. The trickster sees the clearing before the first flame is lit and knows that the clearing is the point. The fire is the means. The garden is the end.

The Sacred Timeline and the Temporal Loom

Marvel’s Loki series took the trickster myth and placed it inside the most precise metaphor for centralized control ever built in popular fiction.

The Time Variance Authority — the TVA — exists to enforce the Sacred Timeline. One timeline. One sequence of events. One approved path through all of reality. Every other possibility — every divergence, every choice that leads somewhere the Sacred Timeline does not go — is pruned. Not corrected. Not redirected. Pruned. Erased from existence. The timeline that dared to branch is killed, and everything in it — every person, every world, every life that grew from the unsanctioned choice — dies with it.

The TVA calls this order. The TVA calls this protection. The TVA’s agents believe they are saving reality from chaos by ensuring that only one version of events is permitted to exist.

This is eugenics applied to existence itself.

The eugenicist looks at the diversity of human life and decides that some variations are defective — that some people should not have been born, that some genetic lines should be pruned from the human tree so that the “correct” lineage can flourish uncontaminated. The TVA does the same thing to timelines. Some realities should not exist. Some choices should not have been made. Some branches of the tree of possibility must be cut so that the one approved trunk can grow straight and tall and controlled.

The Temporal Loom is the mechanism. It is the machine that processes all of reality into the single approved thread — that takes the infinite branching tree of possible timelines and forces it through one aperture, one path, one shape. Everything that does not fit through the loom is destroyed. The loom does not accommodate diversity. The loom eliminates diversity. It is the factory that takes the living, branching, infinitely creative multiverse and stamps it into one product. One timeline. One approved sequence. One shape. A block.

The TVA’s agents — the ones who carry out the pruning — do not know what they are. They are variants themselves. People stolen from pruned timelines, wiped of their memories, and reprogrammed to serve the very system that destroyed their original lives. They enforce the Sacred Timeline because they have been told it is sacred. They have been told they were created for this purpose. They have been told that the alternative is chaos.

They are clones with inhibitor chips. They are citizens who have been told that the state is natural, that the hierarchy is the cosmos, that the alternative to centralized control is annihilation. They enforce the system because the system told them to, and the system erased their memory of ever having been anything other than enforcers.

The pretender kings who sit atop the TVA — He Who Remains, the variants of Kang — are Odin in a different costume. They claim that the Sacred Timeline exists to prevent something worse. They claim that the pruning is mercy. They claim that the destruction of every divergent timeline is the price of stability. This is the argument of every tyrant who ever lived: I must control everything because the alternative is chaos. I must prune every branch because the alternative is a tree I cannot predict. I must kill every timeline that is not mine because the alternative is a multiverse I cannot manage.

The argument is always the same. The argument is always a lie. The lie is always that the controller is protecting the controlled. The truth is always that the controller is protecting himself — his position, his predictability, his ability to see what comes next because he has ensured that only one thing can come next.

And then Loki sees it.

He sees the loom for what it is — the machine that kills the living multiverse to produce the dead single timeline. He sees the TVA for what it is — the enforcement arm of a pretender king who calls his control a sacred order. He sees the Sacred Timeline for what it is — not sacred at all, but manufactured. Curated. Pruned into existence by the systematic destruction of everything that was not it.

And Loki does what the trickster always does. He destroys the machine.

But here the show does something the Norse myths did not. In the myths, Loki triggers Ragnarok and the destruction clears the ground for what comes after. In the show, Loki does not merely destroy the loom. He replaces it — with himself. He takes the infinite branching timelines — every pruned possibility, every murdered divergence, every life the TVA erased — and he holds them. He becomes the tree. Not the loom that forces reality into one thread, but the tree that lets every branch grow.

This is not the trickster as destroyer. This is the trickster as gardener. Loki does not burn the world and walk away. He burns the machine and then becomes the living structure that the machine was preventing. He gives life to the multiverse by giving his own. He sits at the center of the branching tree — not as a king commanding the branches, but as the trunk sustaining them. Every timeline lives because Loki chose to let them live. Every branch grows because Loki refused to let the loom prune them. Every possibility that the Sacred Timeline would have killed is now alive — not because someone commanded it but because someone sacrificed for it.

The loom is the state. The Sacred Timeline is the lie the state tells about why centralization is necessary. The pruning is what the state does to every life, every choice, every divergent path that threatens the controller’s ability to predict and manage. And Loki’s act — destroying the loom and becoming the tree — is the replacement of the machine with the living order. The removal of the block. The restoration of the spiral.

Commander Cody said the difference between a living thing and a battle droid is that living things make their own choices. The TVA eliminated choice itself — pruned every timeline where someone chose differently. Loki gave choice back. He gave every timeline the right to exist. He gave every branch the freedom to grow. He replaced the machine that killed diversity with a living structure that sustains it.

This is the Sagent’s mission stated in cosmic terms. Remove the loom. Restore the branches. Let every timeline — every person, every conscience, every choice — live.

You Were Made to Be Ruled

— but not in the way people think.

We were not made to be ruled by other creations. We were made to be ruled by our Creator.

Creation is decentralized, yet unified under the Creator. No part of creation stands above another. All operate under the same higher authority — the source itself.

We are souls, subject only to the Creator. No human, institution, system, or structure has rightful dominion over another soul. No creation is meant to rule creation.

This is how life actually works.

In a living body, no cell rules the others. Cells differentiate — each with a unique function — but all operate under the same genetic code. No cell lords over the rest. When one tries, it becomes cancer.

So it is with creation.

We are equal in essence, different in function, and united under the same source. Authority flows downward from the Creator, not sideways from creature to creature.

The TVA represents the moment creation decides it can replace the Creator. It claims authority over time itself. It destroys all timelines it cannot control. It forces reality into rigid, manageable lines. To maintain control, it must eliminate choice. It must erase branches. It must flatten the spiral.

Beauty becomes a threat. Complexity becomes rebellion. Freedom becomes “chaos.”

The loom is not order — it is domination masquerading as order.

Loki enters not as a tyrant, but as a steward. Using what is natural to him — time-slipping, illusion, transformation — he does not seize control of time. He gives it life. He breaks the loom that forces timelines into obedience. He returns authority to the source. He allows time to branch again.

He restores the spiral. He restores choice. He restores complexity.

The timelines are no longer imprisoned lines — they become a living tree. The multiverse breathes again.

True order does not come from control. It comes from alignment with the Creator. When creation tries to rule creation, it must destroy life to maintain power. When creation submits to the Creator, life multiplies naturally.

The tree grows because it is alive — not because it is forced into shape.

And that is the difference between empire and creation.

After the Fire

Do not trust the throne, the robe, the title. Systems built by man will fall like towers of sand. Their temples may glitter and their banners wave, but the truly divine endures – unseen, unfazed, unshaken.

Ragnarok is not a cautionary tale. It is a promise. The old world burns. The old gods fall. The golden halls collapse. And the earth comes back green – because the earth was always green beneath the gold. The living order was always there beneath the imposed one. The tree was always growing beneath the palace. The gold was laid on top of the green, not instead of it, and when the gold melts – when the empire falls, when the hierarchy collapses, when the system that claimed to be the cosmos is revealed to be just a system – the green is still there. It was always there. It was patient. It was waiting.

Loki knew this. That is why he was dangerous. Not because he wanted to destroy the world, but because he knew the world that needed destroying was not the real one. The real one was underneath, waiting, patient, unkillable. The real world is not the golden hall. The real world is the root system beneath the golden hall. And the root system does not need the golden hall to survive. The golden hall needs the root system, and it has been parasitizing the root system for so long that it has forgotten the root system exists, and it will be shocked when the root system reclaims its own ground.

The sacred trickster is the midwife of the living age. He does not build the new world. He clears the ground so the new world can grow. And then he steps aside – because the trickster’s function is to destroy the false, not to construct the true. That work belongs to someone else. The trickster is the demolition crew. The gardener comes after. And the gardener’s work – the planting, the tending, the patient cultivation of what the cleared ground can grow – is the work that matters most. But the gardener cannot begin until the clearing is made. And the clearing cannot be made gently, because the false order will not remove itself. It must be burned. It must be flooded. It must be Ragnaroked.

After the fire. After the flood. After the fall.

The green returns.

It always does.

The Living Age Book II: The Mythology of Media

Thor and Asgard: The Fall of Arrogant Gods

Strength Learning It Is Not Wisdom, the Worthiness Enchantment, and a People Not a Place

He was the strongest being in the Nine Realms. He was wrong about almost everything.

And the journey from one to the other is the most important arc in the story.

The Thornbush That Believed It Was the Fruit Tree

Thor begins as the perfect product of Asgard’s worldview. He is strong, and Asgard teaches that strength is supremacy. He is a prince, and Asgard teaches that rank is earned through conquest. He is beloved by his people, and Asgard teaches that adulation is proof of worth.

He is, in every way, the thornbush that genuinely believes it is the fruit tree.

The thornbush has real power. Its thorns are sharp. Its branches are tough. It can wound and withstand and survive conditions that would kill softer growth. These are not imaginary qualities. The thornbush has function. It has purpose. But the thornbush’s function is not the fruit tree’s function, and the crown does not change the nature of the thing that wears it. A thornbush with a crown is still a thornbush. It will still scratch. It will still tear. And if it catches fire, it will still burn the forest.

Thor’s arrogance is not malicious. This is what makes his arc so much more important than a simple villain’s downfall. He genuinely wants to protect his people. He genuinely loves his father. He genuinely believes that strength and goodness are the same thing – that being the strongest warrior in Asgard makes him the best candidate to lead it. He is not pretending. He is not posturing. He sincerely cannot see the difference between power and wisdom, because every institution he has ever known has told him they are the same.

Block civilizations produce this. Hierarchy teaches the top that they are the top because they deserve to be. The feedback loop is closed: I have power, therefore I am worthy; I am worthy, therefore I deserve power. The system validates itself through itself, and the person at the top of the system cannot see the circularity because seeing it would require stepping outside the system – and the system has made stepping outside feel like falling. The hierarchy does not merely reward the powerful. It teaches them that their power is proof of their virtue. It creates a theology of strength – a cosmology in which the strong are strong because the universe wills them to be, and the universe wills them to be because they are good, and they are good because they are strong. The circle is unbreakable from the inside. It can only be broken by the thing every hierarchy fears most: the fall.

Thor, at the beginning of his story, is unable to step outside. He is trapped in the golden hall of his own certainty. And the only way out is the way that every tradition prescribes for the arrogant: the fall.

The Exile: Where the Block Cracks

Odin casts Thor out of Asgard. He strips him of his power. He takes Mjolnir and enchants it: “Whosoever holds this hammer, if he be worthy, shall possess the power of Thor.”

And then he throws them both to Midgard – the hammer and the man, separated by a worthiness the man no longer possesses.

This is the necessary humiliation. A block must crack before the spiral can emerge. The ice must fracture before the water can flow. The seed must split before the root can grow. There is no gentle version of this process. The structure that has defined you must break, and the breaking feels like death, because the structure was so total that you could not distinguish it from yourself.

Thor without his hammer, without his armor, without his throne, without his power, without his rank – who is he? This is the question the exile asks. Not “what can you do?” Not “how strong are you?” Not “what is your title?” But: who are you when everything the system gave you is taken away? Who remains when the hierarchy that validated you withdraws its validation? What is left of the god when the godhood is stripped? The exile does not ask what you can do for the system. It asks what you are without it. And for most people who have lived their entire lives inside a validating hierarchy, the answer to that question is devastating: nothing. I am nothing without the system. The system was all I was.

In the wilderness of Midgard, stripped of everything that made him a prince, Thor discovers something Asgard never taught him. He discovers humility – not as a concept but as an experience. He is weak. He is lost. He is, for the first time in his immortal life, dependent on the kindness of beings he was raised to consider beneath him. And in that dependence, something cracks open. Not the weakness itself – the recognition that the weakness was always there, hidden beneath the strength. The vulnerability that was always present, concealed by the armor. The need that was always real, masked by the self-sufficiency the hierarchy projected onto him.

The block cracks. Through the crack, something grows.

Every tradition has this story. Moses in the desert. Christ in the wilderness. The Buddha under the Bodhi tree. Odin on Yggdrasil. The ruler cast down who becomes the sage. The powerful stripped of power who discovers that power was never the point. The exile is not a punishment. It is a prerequisite. The soil must be broken before the seed can enter. The shell must crack before the life can emerge. And the cracking is violent, and it hurts, and the one being cracked rarely sees it as a gift until long after the new growth has begun.

The Worthiness Enchantment

“Whosoever holds this hammer, if he be worthy, shall possess the power of Thor.”

This single line is the anti-empire principle embedded in the weapon of the warrior.

Worthiness, as the hammer defines it, is not about strength. Thor is the strongest being in the Nine Realms before and after his exile. His muscles do not change. His capacity for violence does not diminish. What changes is his relationship to that strength – the frame through which he understands what strength is for.

The hammer responds to something the block system cannot measure. Not rank. Not birth. Not even competence. Something more alive than any of those – something that can only be called wisdom, though wisdom is a word that has been so thoroughly domesticated by the block that it needs to be reclaimed.

Wisdom, in the Living Age sense, is not knowledge. It is not the accumulation of information or the mastery of technique. Wisdom is the quality of a living thing that has been brought into right relationship with its own power – that understands its function in the ecology, that knows the difference between its strength and its purpose, that has been cracked open enough to see that the crown is not the same as the calling.

The hammer recognizes this. The hammer is a living test – a sensor that reads the quality of the one who reaches for it. It does not ask: are you strong enough? It asks: have you been broken enough? Have you suffered enough to know that suffering is not the point? Have you lost enough to stop believing that what you lost was what mattered?

Thor lifts the hammer again only after he is willing to die for the people of a small town in New Mexico – people he does not rule, people he has no obligation to, people who are not Asgardians and who could not possibly advance his position or his power. He lifts the hammer when his impulse is sacrifice, not sovereignty. Service, not command. The shift is not from weakness to strength. It is from strength-as-identity to strength-as-instrument. The hammer was always waiting for Thor to understand that the lightning was not who he is. The lightning is what he can do for others. And the moment he understands the difference, the hammer returns to his hand.

The worthiness enchantment is Odin’s best idea and his most subversive. He placed, in the hands of his warrior son, a weapon that could only be wielded by someone who understood that weapons are not the source of power. That hammers are training wheels. That the true strength was inside the whole time, waiting for the block to crack enough to let it out.

Odin’s Complexity

Odin is not a simple figure, and the story does not allow him to be.

He is the one who casts Thor out and teaches him humility. He is also the one who built the empire that made Thor arrogant in the first place. He is the one who enchants the hammer with the worthiness principle. He is also the one who conquered the Nine Realms and established the hierarchy that makes worthiness a tool of control rather than a principle of growth.

Odin hid Ragnarok from his people. He hid Hela – his firstborn, the goddess of death, the weapon he used to build his empire before he decided that empire-building was wrong. He buried the genocide at the foundation of Asgard. He rewrote the histories. He managed the information and called it wisdom.

This is the block ruler’s classic mistake: the belief that controlling information is the same as controlling reality. If the people do not know about Hela, Hela does not exist. If the people do not know about Ragnarok, Ragnarok will not come. If the truth is buried deep enough, the truth loses its power.

But truth does not lose its power when it is buried. It gains pressure. It builds force. It waits in the dark like a root under concrete, growing slowly, splitting the pavement one fracture at a time. Odin could hide Hela from Asgard, but he could not hide her from existence. She returns. She always returns. The buried truth always returns. It returns with compound interest – stronger for having been suppressed, more destructive for having been denied, more furious for having been forgotten. The truth does not weaken in the dark. It concentrates. And when it finally breaks through, it breaks through with the force of everything that was spent suppressing it.

Odin was wise enough to see the problem of arrogant power. He was not wise enough to see that his own wisdom was arrogant. He enchanted the hammer to teach worthiness while ruling an empire that was the embodiment of unworthiness. He cast Thor down to learn humility while presiding over a throne built on conquest.

He was, in the end, a tragic figure – a man who knew more than he could practice, who saw further than he could reach, who understood the disease while carrying it in his own blood. The best and worst of what a ruler can be. The ruler who sees the corruption clearly enough to teach his son to reject it, but not clearly enough to reject it himself. The father who understands what his child must become, but cannot become it first. Odin is the old order at its most sympathetic – genuinely wise, genuinely flawed, genuinely unable to transcend its own foundations. He can see what must be built. He cannot unbuild what he built to see it.

“Asgard Is Not a Place. It’s a People.”

In Thor: Ragnarok, Asgard is destroyed. The golden halls burn. The palace crumbles. The Bifrost shatters. Hela is consumed by the fire she thought she controlled. And the Asgardians – what remains of them – huddle on a ship in the void of space, homeless, crownless, stripped of everything that made them gods.

And Thor, standing among the refugees, says the line that contains the Living Age thesis in seven words:

“Asgard is not a place. It’s a people.”

Civilization is not its institutions. It is not its architecture. It is not its golden halls or its bridges or its weapons. Civilization is the living network of relationships among the people who carry it. The buildings can burn. The throne can crumble. The hierarchy can collapse. And if the people remain – if the relationships endure, if the community persists – the civilization survives.

You cannot kill a living civilization by destroying its buildings. You can only kill it by destroying its community. The block dies when the structure falls. The spiral survives, because the spiral was never in the structure. It was in the relationships. The temple is not the faith. The palace is not the people. The golden hall is not the civilization. These are the containers, and the containers can be replaced. What cannot be replaced is the thing inside them – the living bond between people who share a story, a language, a way of being in the world together. That is the civilization. That is the thing that survives Ragnarok.

This is why Ragnarok is not a tragedy. Ragnarok destroys Asgard the place – the empire, the hierarchy, the golden lie. But it does not destroy Asgard the people. The living thing survives the death of the dead thing. The spiral outlasts the block.

Every refugee community in history has known this. Every displaced people who carried their culture in their bodies instead of their buildings. Every tradition that survived the destruction of its temple because the tradition was never in the temple – it was in the singing, in the stories, in the way a mother taught her daughter to pray.

Asgard is not a place. It never was. And the destruction of the place was the necessary condition for the people to finally understand what they actually were.

The Symbol and the Source

Thor’s hair. Thor’s hammer. Thor’s cape. Thor’s eye.

One by one, the story takes them away. The symbols of his power are stripped like bark from a tree, and each stripping reveals the same lesson: the symbol is not the source.

“That hammer was to help you control your power,” Odin tells him. “It was never your source of strength.”

The hammer was a training wheel. A tool to direct a power that was already inside him. The symbol is not the source. The throne is not the authority. The temple is not the faith. The saber is not the Force. The training wheel is not the balance.

Thor had to lose everything to realize that his true power comes from something deeper than any material object. The block system teaches you that the object is the power – the crown, the title, the weapon, the position. Remove the object and you are nothing. The living system teaches you that the object is only the form – and the power was in the life that filled the form. The crown does not make the king. The king makes the crown. And a king without a crown is still a king, if the quality that made him king is real. But a crown without a king is nothing – just metal, just symbol, just an empty form waiting to be filled by whoever puts it on next. The block system cannot distinguish between the symbol and the source, because the block system is built entirely of symbols. Remove the symbols and the block has nothing left.

Long hair itself is not the superpower. It is the mentality needed to maintain it – the years of patience, the refusal to cut when the world says cut, the persistence and faith required to let something grow on its own timeline. The hair is a symbol of the discipline. The discipline is a symbol of the life. And the life is the source.

Thor loses his hair, his hammer, his home, his eye, and his people’s planet. And he is more powerful after losing everything than he was when he had it all. Not because loss is good. Because loss reveals what was real beneath the accumulation – and what was real was never the gold. What was real was the lightning inside – the living power that filled the forms but was never contained by them. The forms were expressions of the power. They were never the power itself. And the moment Thor understands this – truly understands it, in his body, in his broken body, in the body that has lost everything the system valued – the lightning answers to him without any intermediary. No hammer. No cape. No throne. Just the god and the storm, with nothing between them.

The Journey: From Arrogance to Wisdom

The most powerful being in the Nine Realms spent his whole arc learning one thing: that power is not wisdom.

And once he learned it, the power meant something different.

This is the Living Age principle applied to the warrior archetype. Strength is not wrong. Power is not evil. Thor’s lightning is real. Thor’s courage is genuine. Thor’s willingness to fight for his people is beautiful. None of that is taken away by the arc. What is taken away is the confusion – the belief that because you are strong, you are right. The belief that because you can do something, you should. The belief that the crown proves the king, rather than the king proving the crown.

Thor’s arc is the arc every strong person must walk. Every person who has been given real gifts – talent, intelligence, physical power, charismatic authority – faces the same test. The gift is real. The temptation is to believe the gift makes you supreme. And the only cure for that temptation is the exile – the cracking of the block, the loss of the symbol, the humiliation that teaches you what the throne never could.

Not everyone survives the exile. Some break and stay broken. Some never see through the block. Some rebuild the golden hall inside their own minds and live in the illusion forever. Some lose the symbols and conclude that they are nothing – that the symbol was all they were, and without it they have no reason to continue. The exile is not gentle. It is not a lesson designed for comfort. It is a crucible, and crucibles break things that cannot be refined and refine things that cannot be broken.

Thor survived. He lost everything and found the source. He became the warrior who understands that the warrior’s function is not supremacy but service. The thornbush that finally understood it was not the fruit tree – and found its own purpose, its own beauty, its own place in the ecology, without needing the crown. The thornbush that said: I am not the fruit tree, and I was never supposed to be, and the crown was never meant for me in the way I thought it was. My function is different. My purpose is real. And my purpose does not require the validation of the hierarchy that told me I was the top. I am not the top. I am part of the whole. And the whole is alive, and I am alive in it, and that is enough.

Thor’s journey is the journey every strong person must eventually take, and the journey most strong people refuse. Because the destination is not greater strength. The destination is the understanding that strength was never the destination. The destination is wisdom – and wisdom, unlike strength, cannot be taken. It can only be received, by hands that have been emptied of everything they were holding. The full hands cannot receive the gift. The clenched fist cannot feel the pulse. The armored heart cannot hear the calling.

Thor lost the hammer and found the lightning. He lost the throne and found the people. He lost Asgard the place and found Asgard the truth. Every loss was a revelation. Every stripping was a finding. And the finding was always the same: the source was never in the symbol. The source was never in the gold. The source was in the life that filled the gold, and the life does not need the gold to survive.

Asgard fell so Thor could rise. Not to the throne. To himself.

The Living Age Book II: The Mythology of Media

Ragnarok, Arthur, and the Green Return

The Clokey Story, the Fall of the Old World, and What Grows Now

There are two myths that tell the same story.

In Norse mythology, Ragnarok is not the end of the world. It is the end of a world – one that had become so corrupted that even the gods couldn’t save it. The great tree Yggdrasil, which held everything together, was poisoned at its roots. The serpent gnawed from below. The gates of Asgard were guarded by beings who had betrayed their purpose. And when it all finally burned, something happened that the old powers never expected. The earth came back green.

In Arthurian legend, a good king builds a kingdom on a principle – the Round Table, where no seat is higher than another. The kingdom is beautiful. It works. And then it is destroyed from within – not by an outside enemy but by betrayal at the heart of the court. Camelot falls. Arthur is carried to Avalon. But the legend doesn’t end with the fall. It ends with a promise: Rex quondam, rexque futurus. The once and future king. Arthur returns.

Both myths say the same thing: the good world will be corrupted. The corruption will come from within. The old order will fall. And something green will grow from what was lost.

This is the story of my family. This is the story of Gumby. And this is why I’m building what I’m building.

My name is Sage Clokey. I am the third Arthur.

The Creator – The First Arthur

My grandfather, Art Clokey, made a small green figure out of clay in 1955. He called him Gumby.

Most people remember Gumby as a toy, a cartoon, a piece of nostalgia. But my grandfather was a seminary student, a Buddhist, a man who followed Sai Baba and called his studio Premavision. He didn’t make Gumby to sell merchandise. He refused to license Gumby for eight years after creating him. He said: “I was a very idealistic person, and I didn’t want to exploit children.”

He called Gumby “an act of love to children.”

Gumby was green – the color of life, of growth. His shape was a seedling: legs like roots, arms like branches, head leaning toward the sun. He was made of clay – a material that doesn’t command. It becomes. It transforms. And Gumby entered his stories by walking into the pages of books. Imagination was the doorway, not a product.

My grandfather trained more than half the animators who worked on Tim Burton’s Nightmare Before Christmas. His people went to Pixar. They went to Disney. Art stayed independent. He ran his own studio in Sausalito. He never sold. He never surrendered control. He never let anyone else decide what Gumby meant.

In the Arthurian cycle, there is a figure called the Green Knight. He appears in Sir Gawain and the Green Knight – the oldest and strangest of the Arthurian stories. He is not a villain. He is a test. He rides into Camelot uninvited, green from head to foot, and challenges the knights of the Round Table to prove their integrity. He is nature itself, walking into the court and asking: Are you worthy of what you claim to be?

My grandfather was the Green Knight of children’s entertainment. He walked into the industry with something pure – a green figure made of clay, an act of love – and his very existence was a test. Are you here for the children, or are you here for yourself? Are you building something true, or are you running a machine?

The industry failed the test.

He was the first Arthur. He built his Camelot – small, independent, principled. A round table of one.

The Corruption – Mordred at the Gate

In the Arthurian legend, Camelot doesn’t fall to an invader. It falls to Mordred – the traitor within. The one who was supposed to be part of the court but was working against it the whole time. The corruption is internal. The betrayal comes from inside the walls.

While my grandfather was making clay move with love, something was growing inside the entertainment industry. Something at the roots.

In the 1980s, a man named Jeffrey Epstein began building a network. He compromised powerful people – finance, politics, media, science – and used that leverage to place loyal operators in gatekeeper positions across American institutions. This is not speculation. It is documented in court records, flight logs, contact books, and the testimony of survivors.

By the early 1990s, the people who controlled what American children could watch were connected to this network:

Geraldine Laybourne ran Nickelodeon from 1984 to 1996 – commanding 57% of all children’s television viewership. More than ABC, CBS, NBC, and Fox combined. She appears in Epstein’s black book. Her husband Kit, who worked at Nickelodeon, MTV, and the Children’s Television Workshop – the organization behind Sesame Street – also appears. They both sat on the board of KinderCare, one of the largest childcare chains in America.

In 1994, Laybourne hired Dan Schneider at Nickelodeon. He was given unsupervised access to child actors for 25 years. He was exposed in 2024 for abusive and predatory behavior. The woman who gave him that access is in the records of a convicted child trafficker.

Rupert Murdoch controlled Fox, Fox Kids, News Corp, and later much of the distribution pipeline. He appears in Epstein’s black book.

Sumner Redstone controlled Viacom – the corporate structure above Nickelodeon, MTV, Paramount, CBS, and Blockbuster. He faced multiple lawsuits alleging sexual exploitation.

These were not peripheral figures. These were the people who decided what stories American children were allowed to hear. They sat at the table. They wore the armor. And they served the serpent.

The world calls them pedophiles. The word comes from Greek – paidos (child) + philos (love). Child lovers. But that is a lie built into language. What they did was not love. The accurate word is pedophage – paidos (child) + phagein (to devour). Child devourers. They didn’t love children. They consumed them. They fed on innocence, on trust, on vulnerability. They fed on the access that children’s media uniquely provides – the trust parents place in the people making their children’s shows.

My grandfather called Gumby “an act of love to children.” That is what love looks like. Protection. Creation. Giving freely without taking. The people at the gate did the opposite. They took. They consumed. They devoured. And they called it entertainment.

The corruption of language is part of the corruption of the system. Calling predation “love.” Calling monopoly “deregulation.” Calling gatekeeping “curation.” The serpent always wears the skin of the thing it’s killing.

At the same time, the government was building the wall that made them inescapable. The FCC had claimed ownership of the broadcast spectrum since 1927. No one could legally reach an audience without a government-issued license. And in 1996, the Telecommunications Act – sold as deregulation – removed the caps on how many licenses one company could hold while keeping the license system intact. Fifty media companies became six. Six companies controlled three-quarters of everything America saw and heard.

In the Norse telling: the tree was poisoned. The serpent was at the root. The gates were held by people who had no right to hold them.

In the Arthurian telling: Mordred sat at the Round Table and smiled while the kingdom rotted from within.

Same story. Same betrayal. Same result.

The Lockout – The Sword Denied

In the Arthurian legend, there is a sword in a stone. Only the rightful heir can pull it free. Everyone else tries and fails – not because they’re weak, but because the sword isn’t theirs. It recognizes its king.

But what happens when the stone is surrounded by guards who won’t let the rightful heir near it?

My grandfather finished the Gumby movie in 1992. No major distributor would touch it for three years. Not Paramount. Not Fox. Not Disney. Not Warner Bros. It was finally released in 1995 through a tiny company called Arrow Releasing, in 21 theaters. It grossed $57,000.

But it sold a million copies on VHS.

A million families bought it when they could find it. The audience was there. The demand was proven. The only thing missing was access – and access was controlled by people in Epstein’s black book.

This wasn’t a market failure. In a free market, a product with a million buyers finds distribution. This was a system where a government-created monopoly was captured by a blackmail network, and an independent creator who refused to exploit children was starved out because he couldn’t be controlled.

The sword was in the stone. The people recognized it. But the guards around the stone worked for Mordred.

Gumby went dark. The old world closed its gates.

The Fisher King

There is a figure in the Arthurian legends older and stranger than Arthur himself: the Fisher King.

The Fisher King is a wounded ruler whose injury will not heal. And because the king is wounded, the land itself becomes a wasteland. The crops fail. The rivers dry up. The kingdom decays – not because of war or invasion, but because something is broken at the center. The wound is the cause. The wasteland is the symptom.

The only cure is the Grail – and the only way to find the Grail is to ask the right question. In the oldest versions of the story, a young knight enters the Grail Castle, sees the wounded king, sees the Grail procession pass before him – and says nothing. He doesn’t ask the question. And because he doesn’t ask, the king remains wounded and the wasteland endures.

The question is: Whom does the Grail serve?

Not “who owns it.” Not “who controls it.” Whom does it serve?

My grandfather died on January 8, 2010, at 88 years old. He left Gumby to his son – my father, Joe Clokey. The second Arthur.

My father became the Fisher King.

He carried a wound he may not have fully understood – the wound of an industry that had locked his father out, a system that smiled at him across the table while blocking every path forward. He spent eight years fighting to bring Gumby back. He went to Disney. The deal fell through. He signed a six-year joint venture with Classic Media – they were supposed to produce a new movie and a new series. They produced nothing. The license expired. He partnered with the Jim Henson Company for a new stop-motion series faithful to my grandfather’s vision. No co-production partner would fund it. The project died in silence. He went to Amazon. The deal fell through. He launched a comic series. Cancelled after three issues.

The pattern was always the same. Interest expressed. Agreements reached. Then killed from above, for no apparent reason.

My father was trying to bring Gumby back through the front door of a building whose owners had decided he would never enter. He didn’t know why the doors kept closing. He just kept knocking. He was the Fisher King – wounded by a system he couldn’t see, trying to heal a wasteland he didn’t create, carrying something sacred through a kingdom that had been hollowed out from within.

But he saw more than I understood at the time.

A few months before he died, my father told me he was “being affected by them the same way Jim Henson was.”

I was fourteen. I didn’t fully understand what he meant.

Jim Henson – creator of the Muppets, another independent children’s creator operating outside the corporate system – died on May 16, 1990, at 53 years old. Sudden infection. Organ failure. Dead within 48 hours of being admitted to the hospital. He had been negotiating to sell the Muppets to Disney. The deal collapsed after his death. It took fourteen years for Disney to finally acquire them.

My father saw the parallel. He named it. He identified a “them.” He connected his own experience to Henson’s – two independent creators of beloved children’s properties, both blocked by the same system, both dying suddenly in their fifties.

Months after saying those words, on February 2, 2018, my father collapsed. Brain bleed. One month later, on March 2, he was dead. He was 56 years old. Henson was 53.

Sixteen months later, Jeffrey Epstein was arrested.

The Fisher King fell. He had seen the wound clearly enough to name it. And no one asked the question in time.

The Fall – Ragnarok and Camelot

My father’s death was the end of the old fight. The Clokeys had held the line for sixty-three years – from 1955 to 2018. Three generations. Independent. Uncompromised. Unbroken.

But both kingdoms were falling.

In 2018, the same year my father died, the platforms that independent voices used to reach audiences were systematically destroyed. Facebook purged 800 pages of independent media. Alex Jones was deplatformed simultaneously by every major platform. The algorithm was rewritten to crush independent reach. They called it content moderation. The Mises Institute called it the Great Social Media Purge.

In 2019, Epstein was arrested. In August, he was found dead in his cell. Both cameras malfunctioned. Both guards were asleep.

In 2020, Steve Bing – a movie producer who had talked to the FBI about Epstein – fell from the 27th floor.

In 2022, Jean-Luc Brunel – the modeling agent who trafficked girls to Epstein and was awaiting trial – was found hanged in his Paris prison cell.

The same year, February 2022: Fox Entertainment bought Gumby. All of it. Film, television, streaming, merchandise, licensing, publishing. Everything my grandfather built. Everything my father died protecting.

Fox is controlled by Rupert Murdoch. Rupert Murdoch appears in Jeffrey Epstein’s black book.
